topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aspects of Human-Machine Systems: A Model of Human Information Processing; Process of Seeing; Visual Capabilities - Accommodation, Visual Acuity, and Contrast Sensitivity; Factors Affecting Visual Capabilities; Human Factor Aspects of Hard Copy Text and Computer Screen Text; Graphic Representations of Text & Data; Human Factors in Designing Symbols & Codes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Human-Machine Systems (HMS)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A **Human-Machine System (HMS)** is an integrated socio-technical configuration in which a human operator and a machine/computer subsystem interact cooperatively to accomplish a defined task or goal, exchanging information, energy, and control through well-defined interfaces.

In the framework of **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)** — also termed *Ergonomics* — the system is decomposed into the **Human Subsystem (H)**, the **Machine Subsystem (M)**, and the **Environment (E)**. Performance is judged not just by machine efficiency, but by the **compatibility** between the operator's sensory, cognitive, and motor capabilities and the machine's display/control layout.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Examiners frequently test the **three-sub-system model** (H–M–E) and the **compatibility principle** in 2-mark and 3-mark questions.

#### 1.1.1 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are driving a car on a busy highway. The car (machine) has a speedometer (display), a steering wheel (control), and brake pedals (control). Your eyes detect signs, the engine's RPM, and surrounding vehicles (perception). Your brain decides when to brake, accelerate, or change lanes (cognition). Your hands and feet actuate the controls (motor response). The road, weather, and traffic form the **Environment**. The car cannot drive itself efficiently without you, and you cannot navigate effectively without the car's instruments. This reciprocal coupling is a **Human-Machine System**.

---

### 1.2 A Model of Human Information Processing

The most widely accepted framework in HFE for describing how humans handle information is the **Information Processing Model (IPM)**, classically attributed to **Broadbent (1958)** and later refined by **Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory (MRT)** and **Card, Moran & Newell's Model Human Processor (MHP, 1983)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Human Information Processing refers to the sequence of cognitive operations by which a human operator detects sensory stimuli, encodes them, processes them in working memory, decides on a response, and executes motor actions, all under bounded cognitive capacity.

The canonical pipeline is:

$$
\text{Stimulus} \longrightarrow \text{Sensory Register} \longrightarrow \text{Perception} \longrightarrow \text{Working Memory} \longrightarrow \text{Decision Making} \longrightarrow \text{Response Selection} \longrightarrow \text{Motor Action} \longrightarrow \text{Response}
$$

#### 1.2.1 Stages Explained with Analogies

1. **Sensory Register (Iconic/Echoic Memory):** Think of it as a *high-speed camera buffer* that briefly holds raw sensory data (≈ 250 ms for vision, ≈ 2 s for hearing). If not attended to, the trace decays.
2. **Perception:** The brain's "filter and pattern-matching unit" that organizes raw sensory input into meaningful objects (e.g., recognizing a red light as a stop signal).
3. **Working Memory (Short-Term Memory, STM):** A *mental scratchpad* with limited capacity (**Miller's Law: 7 ± 2 chunks**, later refined to **4 ± 1 chunks** by Cowan). Holds information for ≈ 20–30 seconds without rehearsal.
4. **Long-Term Memory (LTM):** A *vast semantic library* storing schemas, rules, and procedures. Retrieval is cue-dependent.
5. **Decision Making:** Comparing perceived state with LTM-stored goals and rules to select a response.
6. **Response Selection & Motor Action:** Translating the decision into muscular commands (Fitts' Law governs aiming movements).

#### 1.2.2 Intuitive Analogy for the IPM

Consider a **pilot in a cockpit**:
- The **radar screen flicker** (stimulus) briefly registers on the retina (sensory register).
- The pilot **interprets** the blip as a target (perception).
- The blip's coordinates are held in mind (working memory).
- Cross-referenced with **stored training procedures** (LTM), a maneuver is decided (decision).
- The pilot's **hand moves the joystick** (motor action).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The *Model Human Processor (MHP)* proposes three interacting processors — **Perceptual, Cognitive, and Motor** — each characterized by **cycle time (τ)** and **memory capacity**. Wickens extended this with **resources** (visual, auditory, spatial, verbal) that are partially independent, allowing parallel processing if tasks use different resources.

---

### 1.3 Process of Seeing (The Visual Pathway)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The *Process of Seeing* (Visual Perception) is the physiological and neurological sequence by which electromagnetic radiation in the **visible spectrum (≈ 380–780 nm)** is captured by the eye, transduced into neural signals, and interpreted by the brain as form, color, depth, and motion.

#### 1.3.1 Anatomical & Optical Pathway

The light path through the eye is:

$$
\text{Cornea} \longrightarrow \text{Aqueous Humor} \longrightarrow \text{Pupil (Iris)} \longrightarrow \text{Lens} \longrightarrow \text{Vitreous Humor} \longrightarrow \text{Retina (Rods \& Cones)} \longrightarrow \text{Optic Nerve} \longrightarrow \text{Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN)} \longrightarrow \text{Primary Visual Cortex (V1)}
$$

- **Cornea:** Provides ≈ **2/3 of the eye's total refractive power (≈ 43 dioptres)**.
- **Pupil/Iris:** Adjusts aperture (≈ 2 mm bright → 7 mm dark) to control retinal illuminance.
- **Lens:** Variable-power element (≈ **15–20 D**) that fine-tunes focus.
- **Retina:** Contains ≈ **120 million rods** (scotopic / dim-light, monochromatic) and ≈ **6 million cones** (photopic / bright-light, color-sensitive: S, M, L cones for blue, green, red).

#### 1.3.2 Intuitive Analogy

The eye works like a **camera**: the **cornea + lens** is the lens system, the **pupil** is the aperture, the **retina** is the film/sensor, and the **visual cortex** is the image-processing software that converts raw pixels into meaningful scenes.

---

### 1.4 Visual Capabilities

#### 1.4.1 Accommodation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Accommodation* is the dynamic adjustment of the **lens's optical power** by the ciliary muscle to focus on objects at varying distances, producing a sharp image on the retina.

The **far point** (objects focused on with relaxed lens) is theoretically at infinity, while the **near point** (closest object focused with maximum accommodation) recedes with age — a condition called **Presbyopia**.

$$
\text{Amplitude of Accommodation (D)} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{near (m)}}} - \frac{1}{f_{\text{far (m)}}}
$$

- A **10-year-old**: ≈ **14 D** (near point ≈ 7 cm)
- A **30-year-old**: ≈ **7 D**
- A **60-year-old**: ≈ **1 D** (near point ≈ 1 m)

**Design Implication:** Reading materials and control displays should be placed within the operator's *Zone of Clear Vision* (typically **40–70 cm** for adults) to minimize accommodative strain.

#### 1.4.2 Visual Acuity

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Visual Acuity (VA)* is the angular resolution of the visual system — the smallest angular detail a person can discriminate. It is a measure of *spatial sharpness of vision*.

The clinical standard is the **Snellen Fraction**:

$$
\text{VA} = \frac{\text{Test Distance (D, typically 20 ft or 6 m)}}{\text{Distance at which a normal eye reads the line}}
$$

So **20/20 (6/6)** vision means the subject reads at 20 ft what a standard eye reads at 20 ft. **20/200** is the legal blindness threshold in many jurisdictions.

**Angular Acuity** is also expressed as the **minimum angle of resolution (MAR)**:

$$
\text{MAR} = \frac{1 \text{ arc-minute}}{60} \approx 0.0003 \text{ rad} \approx 1 \text{ arc-minute}
$$

For a 6-m test distance and a 7.5 mm letter height (Snellen "E" 6/6 line), the subtended angle is:

$$
\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{7.5}{6000}\right) \approx 0.072^\circ \approx 4.3 \text{ arc-minutes}
$$

#### 1.4.3 Contrast Sensitivity

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Contrast Sensitivity (CS)* is the reciprocal of the **Contrast Threshold (C_T)** — the minimum luminance difference between a target and its background that an observer can detect.

The **Weber–Fechner Law** defines contrast:

$$
C = \frac{\Delta L}{L_{\text{background}}} = \frac{\vert L_{\text{target}} - L_{\text{background}} \vert}{L_{\text{background}}}
$$

where $\Delta L = L_{\text{target}} - L_{\text{background}}$ and $L$ is luminance (cd/m²).

The **Michelson Contrast** is used for periodic patterns (gratings):

$$
C_{\text{Michelson}} = \frac{L_{\text{max}} - L_{\text{min}}}{L_{\text{max}} + L_{\text{min}}}
$$

**Contrast Sensitivity Function (CSF)** plots CS against **spatial frequency (cycles/degree)**. CS peaks around **2–5 cycles/degree** and falls off at both low and high spatial frequencies (a **band-pass** characteristic).

---

### 1.5 Human-Factor Aspects of Hard Copy vs. Computer Screen Text

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Hard copy refers to text/data rendered on a *physical substrate* (paper, microfilm); computer screen text refers to text/data rendered on a *self-luminous emissive display* (CRT, LCD, OLED).

Key comparative parameters:

| Parameter | Hard Copy (Paper) | Computer Screen (CRT/LCD) |
|---|---|---|
| **Luminance** | Reflective (300–500 cd/m² equiv.) | Emissive (50–300 cd/m² typical) |
| **Resolution** | 300–2400 dpi | 72–218 ppi (typical) |
| **Refresh / Flicker** | None | 60–240 Hz (flicker risk) |
| **Glare** | Matte/gloss surface dependent | Specular + diffuse glare |
| **Viewing distance** | 30–40 cm (held) | 50–70 cm (seated) |
| **Stability of image** | Perfectly stable | Can have jitter, flicker |
| **Ergonomic adjustment** | Limited (page, lighting) | Tilt, height, brightness, contrast |
| **Cumulative fatigue** | Low | Higher (CVS — see below) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer Vision Syndrome (CVS):** Prolonged screen use causes eyestrain, blurred vision, dry eyes, and headaches. The American Optometric Association attributes it to **glare, poor contrast, improper viewing distance, and uncorrected refractive errors**. Design recommendations: 20-20-20 rule (every 20 min, look at 20 ft for 20 s), anti-glare filters, top of screen at or below eye level, viewing distance ≈ **50–70 cm**, and document holders placed adjacent to the screen.

---

### 1.6 Graphic Representations of Text & Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Graphic representations* are visual encodings (charts, graphs, maps, pictograms) that translate quantitative or categorical data into perceivable geometric form, leveraging the visual system's strengths in detecting position, length, area, and color.

Edward Tufte's principles for graphical integrity emphasize the **data-ink ratio**:

$$
\text{Data-Ink Ratio} = \frac{\text{Ink used for data}}{\text{Total ink used in graphic}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cleveland & McGill's Hierarchy of Perceptual Tasks (1984)** — the canonical ranking used in HFE for choosing chart types (most to least accurate): **Position on common scale > Position on non-aligned scale > Length, Direction, Angle > Area > Volume, Curvature > Shading, Color saturation.**

---

### 1.7 Human Factors in Designing Symbols & Codes

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *Symbol* is a perceivable sign that represents a referent by **convention or resemblance**; a *Code* is a *systematic mapping* between a set of symbols and a set of meanings (e.g., semaphore flags, color-coded resistor bands).

A *Pictogram* combines symbol and code properties: a stylized, simplified graphic that resembles the referent (e.g., 🚭 for "no smoking").

Design criteria (per ISO 7001 / ANSI Z535):
1. **Visibility / Detectability**
2. **Legibility / Recognizability**
3. **Understandability / Meaning inference**
4. **Learnability / Memorability**
5. **Cultural neutrality**
6. **Conspicuity under degraded conditions**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Hook:** Always remember **"Symbols save space, codes save time."** A well-designed pictogram transcends language barriers — critical for safety signage in airports, chemical plants, and export-manufactured consumer goods.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Human-Machine System (HMS)

The HFE community (Sanders & McCormick, *Human Factors in Engineering and Design*, classical reference framework) decomposes any HMS into:

1. **Human Subsystem (H):** Sensory, central (cognitive), and motor processors with bounded capacities.
2. **Machine Subsystem (M):** Display, control, and feedback elements.
3. **Environment (E):** Physical (noise, light, temperature), social, and organizational context.
4. **Task:** The goal-directed activity linking H, M, and E.
5. **Interface:** The boundary where H meets M (display-control layout, software UI).

The performance function:

$$
P_{\text{system}} = f(H, M, E, T, \text{Interface})
$$

The HFE goal is to **maximize $P_{\text{system}}$** by improving *compatibility* and *match* between human capabilities and machine demands.

---

### 2.2 Model Human Processor (MHP) — Quantitative Detail

Card, Moran & Newell (1983) parameterize the human as three interacting processors, each with cycle time $\tau$ and decay time $t_d$:

| Processor | Memory Type | Cycle Time $\tau$ | Decay $t_d$ | Capacity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Perceptual** | Sensory registers (iconic, echoic) | $\tau_P \approx 100 \text{ ms}$ | $t_{dP} \approx 250 \text{ ms}$ (iconic) | Large (≥ 17 letters briefly) |
| **Cognitive** | Working memory (STM) | $\tau_C \approx 70 \text{ ms}$ | $t_{dC} \approx 10\text{–}30 \text{ s}$ | ≈ **4 ± 1 chunks** (Cowan) |
| **Motor** | Motor memory (LTM) | $\tau_M \approx 70 \text{ ms}$ | $t_{dM} \approx 200 \text{ ms}$ | Limited by Fitts' Law |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total reaction time (Hick's Law + simple RT):**
> $$RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(N+1)$$
> where $N$ = number of equally likely choices, $a$ ≈ 200 ms (simple RT), $b$ ≈ 100–150 ms/bit.

---

### 2.3 Accommodation: Quantitative Analysis

The crystalline lens is suspended by the **zonular fibers** attached to the **ciliary muscle**.

- **Relaxed state** (looking at distant objects): Zonular fibers taut → lens flattened → **minimum power**.
- **Contracted state** (looking at near objects): Zonular fibers slack → lens bulges → **maximum power**.

**Amplitude of Accommodation** $A$ (in dioptres, D):

$$
A = P_{\text{max}} - P_{\text{min}} = \frac{1}{u_{\text{near}}} - \frac{1}{u_{\text{far}}}
$$

where $u$ is the object distance in meters (positive for real objects). For a normal young adult, $u_{\text{far}} = \infty$ so $P_{\text{min}} = 0$, and $A = 1 / u_{\text{near}}$.

**Duane's curve** (clinical standard) gives age-dependent norms:

| Age (yrs) | Mean Amplitude (D) | Near Point (cm) |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | 14.0 | 7.1 |
| 20 | 11.0 | 9.1 |
| 30 | 7.5 | 13.3 |
| 40 | 5.5 | 18.2 |
| 50 | 2.0 | 50.0 |
| 60 | 1.0 | 100.0 |

**Engineering Design Implication:** Adjustable reading stands, magnifiers, and progressive-power (multifocal) lenses compensate for presbyopic operators in industries like printing, jewelry, electronics assembly.

---

### 2.4 Visual Acuity: Theory and Computation

#### 2.4.1 Snellen Acuity

A Snellen letter is constructed such that each stroke width subtends **1 arc-minute** at the design distance. The full letter subtends **5 arc-minutes** vertically.

For a viewing distance $d$ (m) and letter height $h$ (mm):

$$
\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{h}{1000 \cdot d}\right) \text{ degrees} \approx \frac{57.3 \cdot h}{1000 \cdot d}
$$

**Snellen Ratio:**

$$
R = \frac{d_{\text{test}}}{d_{\text{ref}}}
$$

#### 2.4.2 Minimum Legible / Separable Angles

- **Minimum legible angle:** ≈ 5 arc-minutes (for whole Snellen letter)
- **Minimum separable angle:** ≈ 0.5–1 arc-minute (for Vernier acuity, hyperacuity)
- **Minimum visible angle:** ≈ 0.5 arc-second (theoretical, using a single dot)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters for display design:** The **critical detail** (C) of a display feature and the **viewing distance** (D) must satisfy the **legibility rule of thumb**:
> $$C_{\text{min}} \text{ (mm)} \geq \frac{\theta_{\text{eye, min}} \cdot D}{3438}$$
> For $\theta = 5$ arc-min and $D = 500$ mm, $C_{\text{min}} \approx 0.73$ mm.

#### 2.4.3 LogMAR Notation

Often used clinically:

$$
\text{LogMAR} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d_{\text{test}}}{d_{\text{ref}}}\right)
$$

20/20 = LogMAR 0.0; 20/200 = LogMAR 1.0. Negative LogMAR is *better* than normal.

---

### 2.5 Contrast Sensitivity: Theory and Engineering Utility

#### 2.5.1 Weber's Law and Its Limits

Weber's Law holds for a wide range of photopic luminances (the **Weber fraction** $k \approx 0.01$–$0.02$ for foveal vision):

$$
\Delta L = k \cdot L
$$

Below a certain **dark light threshold** (rod-cone break at ≈ 10⁻³ cd/m²), $\Delta L$ becomes roughly constant (Riccò's region: $L \cdot A = \text{const}$), and at very high luminances, **glare** and **blooming** degrade discrimination.

#### 2.5.2 Contrast Sensitivity Function (CSF)

The CSF is often modeled by a band-pass function (Kelly, 1977):

$$
\text{CSF}(u) = u^n \cdot e^{-k \cdot u}
$$

where $u$ = spatial frequency (cycles/degree), $n \approx 1.5$–$2$, and $k$ adjusts the peak. Maximum CS occurs at **2–5 cycles/degree** and drops off for both very coarse and very fine patterns.

**Engineering utility:**
- **CCTV, medical imaging, X-ray displays, aircraft HUDs:** Must be designed with sufficient contrast to be visible to operators with **20/40 VA** (≈ 90th percentile) and a CSF envelope.
- **Minimum perceptible contrast for displays:** Weber ratio of ≈ **0.05–0.1** is recommended (vs. ideal observer 0.01).

---

### 2.6 Hard Copy vs. Computer Screen Text — Quantitative Comparison

#### 2.6.1 Luminance Requirements

- **Paper (white office paper):** Reflectance ≈ **0.83**, under 500 lux illumination gives $L \approx 130 \text{ cd/m}^2$.
- **LCD (typical):** 200–400 cd/m² (white), 1–3 cd/m² (black), giving **Michelson contrast ≈ 0.99**.
- **Glossy paper:** Mirror-like reflections can produce **veiling glare** of 50–1000 cd/m² under fluorescent lamps, reducing effective contrast.

#### 2.6.2 Character Height and Stroke Width

For 20/20 VA viewing:

$$
\text{Stroke width } s \geq 0.083 \cdot h \text{ (height)}
$$

For a 5 mm character at 500 mm: $s \geq 0.4$ mm.

For 20/40 VA (operator with reduced acuity), double these to ≈ 10 mm and 0.8 mm stroke.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The **preferred character height-to-width ratio** is **1 : 0.7 to 1 : 0.9**. Stroke width **8–12 %** of height. Spacing between characters ≈ **one stroke width**; line spacing ≈ **one character height**.

#### 2.6.3 Viewing Distance

ANSI/HFES 100 standard recommends:
- **VDT primary task:** 50–70 cm
- **Document holder:** same distance as screen, located *adjacent* (not below) to avoid re-focusing fatigue.
- **Reference documents:** within 30–40° of the screen normal.

---

### 2.7 Graphic Representations — Tufte's and Cleveland's Principles

#### 2.7.1 Tufte's Principles (from *The Visual Display of Quantitative Information*)

1. **Show the data** (above all).
2. **Maximize the data-ink ratio.**
3. **Erase non-data ink.**
4. **Erase redundant data ink.**
5. **Revise and edit.**

#### 2.7.2 Cleveland & McGill's Perceptual Ranking

In order of *accuracy* with which humans decode quantitative information:

1. **Position along a common scale** (e.g., dot plot, bar chart on a zero baseline) — most accurate.
2. **Positions on identical but non-aligned scales.**
3. **Length.**
4. **Angle / Slope.**
5. **Area.**
6. **Volume / Curvature.**
7. **Shading / Color hue (with no ordering).** — least accurate.

> [!TIP]
> **Design Rule:** If the goal is *precise comparison* of values, use **bar charts with a zero baseline**. Pie charts are *categorical-share visualizations* and should not be used for fine quantitative comparison because angle and area decoding is poor.

#### 2.7.3 Common Chart Types and Their Use

| Chart Type | Best Used For | Avoid When |
|---|---|---|
| **Bar chart** | Comparing discrete categories | Many categories (> 10) |
| **Line chart** | Trends over continuous time | Categorical or non-ordered x-axis |
| **Scatter plot** | Correlation between two variables | One of the variables is categorical |
| **Pie chart** | Showing part-to-whole ≤ 5 slices | Precise comparison, many slices |
| **Histogram** | Distribution of a continuous variable | Discrete categories |
| **Heat map** | Multi-dimensional density | Color-blind viewers (use viridis colormap) |
| **Pictogram** | Public-facing impact communication | Technical/scientific reports |

---

### 2.8 Symbol and Code Design — Theoretical Framework

#### 2.8.1 Symbol Categories

- **Iconic (Pictographic):** Resembles referent (e.g., 🛗 for elevator).
- **Arbitrary (Conventional):** Convention-based (e.g., skull-and-crossbones for poison).
- **Alphanumeric:** Letters/numbers (e.g., "H₂SO₄").
- **Color codes:** Red = stop/danger, Green = safe/proceed, Yellow = caution, Blue = mandatory action (ANSI Z535 / ISO 3864).

#### 2.8.2 Design Heuristics

1. **Simplicity:** ≤ 5–7 elements per symbol.
2. **Closure:** Solid outlines help in degraded conditions.
3. **Generality:** Avoid culturally specific details.
4. **Color discriminability:** Use **redundant coding** (color + shape + label) to support color-vision-deficient users (≈ **8 % of males, 0.5 % of females** have some form of CVD).
5. **Test for comprehensibility:** ISO 9186 suggests ≥ **85 % comprehension** among target population.

#### 2.8.3 Code Design Principles

- **Distinctiveness:** Code elements must be unambiguously distinguishable.
- **Meaningful mapping:** e.g., higher temperature = more saturated red.
- **Consistency:** Same code → same meaning across the system.
- **Limited set:** Avoid excessive code cardinality (Miller's 7 ± 2 limit applies).

---

### 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Accommodation Amplitude | $A = 1/u_{\text{near}} - 1/u_{\text{far}}$ | Dioptre (D) | $u$ in metres; presbyopia = $A \downarrow$ with age |
| 2 | Angular subtense | $\theta = h / d$ (small angle) | rad or ° | $h$ = height, $d$ = distance |
| 3 | Snellen Ratio | $R = d_{\text{test}} / d_{\text{ref}}$ | dimensionless | 20/20 = normal; 20/200 = legal blindness |
| 4 | LogMAR | $\log_{10}(d_{\text{test}}/d_{\text{ref}})$ | log units | Negative = better than normal |
| 5 | Weber Contrast | $C = \Delta L / L_{\text{bg}}$ | dimensionless | Range 0–1 (or >1 for dark targets) |
| 6 | Michelson Contrast | $(L_{\max} - L_{\min})/(L_{\max} + L_{\min})$ | dimensionless | Used for periodic patterns |
| 7 | Contrast Sensitivity | $CS = 1 / C_T$ | dimensionless | $C_T$ = threshold contrast |
| 8 | CSF band-pass | $CS(u) = u^n e^{-k u}$ | dimensionless | Peak at 2–5 cyc/deg |
| 9 | Simple RT (Hick) | $RT = a + b \log_2(N+1)$ | ms | $a \approx 200$, $b \approx 100\text{–}150$ |
| 10 | Fitts' Law (movement time) | $MT = a + b \log_2(1 + D/W)$ | ms | $D$ = distance, $W$ = width |
| 11 | Reading speed (ideal) | ≈ 250 wpm | words/min | Slower for screen (~180 wpm) |
| 12 | Min char height (legibility) | $h_{\min} \approx 5\text{ arc-min} \cdot d$ | mm | At 500 mm: ≈ 0.73 mm (20/20) |
| 13 | Data-ink ratio | $DI = I_{\text{data}}/I_{\text{total}}$ | dimensionless | Tufte: maximize |
| 14 | Critical detail (display) | $C \geq \theta_{\text{eye}} \cdot d / 3438$ | mm | $\theta$ in arc-min, $d$ in mm |
| 15 | Viewing distance (VDT) | 50–70 cm | cm | ANSI/HFES 100 |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Amplitude of Accommodation from Geometric Optics

**Given:** A human eye with ciliary muscle relaxed (looking at far point) and fully contracted (looking at near point). The lens-to-retina distance (image distance) is effectively constant at ≈ $v \approx 17$ mm ≈ 0.017 m for a schematic reduced eye.

**Step 1 — Recall the thin-lens equation:**

$$
\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{u} + \frac{1}{v}
$$

**Step 2 — For a relaxed eye (far vision):** The object is at infinity, so $u_{\text{far}} = \infty \Rightarrow 1/u_{\text{far}} = 0$. The eye's total power $P_{\text{min}}$ must satisfy:

$$
P_{\text{min}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{far}}} = \frac{1}{\infty} + \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{0.017} \approx 58.8 \text{ D}
$$

**Step 3 — For maximum accommodation (near point at $u_{\text{near}}$):**

$$
P_{\text{max}} = \frac{1}{u_{\text{near}}} + \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{u_{\text{near}}} + 58.8
$$

**Step 4 — Amplitude of Accommodation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= P_{\text{max}} - P_{\text{min}} \\
&= \left(\frac{1}{u_{\text{near}}} + \frac{1}{v}\right) - \left(\frac{1}{u_{\text{far}}} + \frac{1}{v}\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{u_{\text{near}}} - \frac{1}{u_{\text{far}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a normal young adult with $u_{\text{near}} = 0.25$ m (25 cm) and $u_{\text{far}} = \infty$:

$$
A = \frac{1}{0.25} - 0 = 4.0 \text{ D}
$$

A 10-year-old with $u_{\text{near}} = 0.07$ m:

$$
A = \frac{1}{0.07} \approx 14.3 \text{ D}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the constant 1/v:** Since $v$ is unchanged, it cancels out, giving the simple form $A = 1/u_{\text{near}} - 1/u_{\text{far}}$. This is the standard clinical formula.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Snellen Letter Geometry and Angular Acuity

**Given:** A standard Snellen "E" optotype at test distance $d$ from the patient's eye, with reference distance $d_{\text{ref}}$ for a normal eye.

**Step 1 — Design geometry of the Snellen letter:** A "6/6" letter is constructed so that:
- The full letter height $H$ subtends **5 arc-minutes** at $d_{\text{ref}} = 6$ m.
- Each stroke width $s$ subtends **1 arc-minute**.

**Step 2 — Convert 5 arc-minutes to physical height at 6 m:**

$$
H = d_{\text{ref}} \cdot \tan(5') = 6 \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{5\pi}{10800}\right) \approx 6 \cdot 0.001454 \approx 8.73 \text{ mm}
$$

(Standard Snellen "E" is ≈ 8.73 mm tall, with each stroke ≈ 1.745 mm wide.)

**Step 3 — Angular subtense at test distance $d$:**

$$
\theta = \frac{H}{d} = \frac{8.73 \text{ mm}}{d \text{ (mm)}}
$$

**Step 4 — For a normal eye (20/20 = 6/6), $d = 6$ m = 6000 mm:**

$$
\theta_{\text{normal}} = \frac{8.73}{6000} = 0.001455 \text{ rad} = 5' \approx 0.0833^\circ
$$

**Step 5 — For a patient who can just resolve this letter only at $d = 12$ m but normal eye resolves at 6 m:**

$$
\text{VA} = \frac{d_{\text{patient}}}{d_{\text{normal}}} = \frac{12}{6} = 2 \text{ (or 20/40)}
$$

**Step 6 — LogMAR conversion:**

$$
\text{LogMAR} = \log_{10}(2) \approx 0.301
$$

This patient reads 2× worse than normal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Reading Rule:** A Snellen ratio of 6/6 (20/20) corresponds to a MAR of 1 arc-minute. **Most engineering display standards (MIL-STD-1472, ANSI/HFES 100) use 4–5 arc-minutes as the *minimum legible* angle** for operator tasks (e.g., reading a status label), because reading involves *whole-letter recognition*, not just *detection of a single stroke*.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Character Height for Legibility

**Given:** Operator with VA = 20/40 (LogMAR ≈ 0.301) viewing a display at distance $d$ mm.

**Step 1 — Find the patient's MAR:**

$$
\text{MAR}_{\text{patient}} = 1' \times 10^{\text{LogMAR}} = 1' \times 10^{0.301} = 1' \times 2 = 2 \text{ arc-minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Stroke width visibility:** Stroke must subtend ≥ MAR:

$$
s = d \cdot \tan(\text{MAR}) = d \cdot \tan(2')
$$

**Step 3 — Character height (5 strokes per letter height):**

$$
H = 5s = 5 d \tan(2')
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation for $d = 500$ mm:**

$$
s = 500 \cdot \tan(0.0333^\circ) = 500 \cdot 5.82 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.291 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
H = 5 \cdot 0.291 = 1.455 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply 1.5× safety factor (industry convention for safety-critical signage):**

$$
H_{\text{design}} = 1.5 \times 1.455 \approx 2.18 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the safety factor and the underlying assumption (MAR, viewing distance). Examiners reward explicit numerical values, not just abstract "it should be big enough."

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Weber Contrast for a Display

**Given:** A CRT/LCD display has white at $L_W = 250$ cd/m² and black at $L_K = 2$ cd/m².

**Step 1 — Compute Michelson contrast:**

$$
C_{\text{Michelson}} = \frac{L_W - L_K}{L_W + L_K} = \frac{250 - 2}{250 + 2} = \frac{248}{252} \approx 0.984
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Weber contrast for text on white background (e.g., dark text $L_T = 5$ cd/m² on white $L_B = 250$ cd/m²):**

$$
C_{\text{Weber}} = \frac{\vert L_B - L_T \vert}{L_B} = \frac{\vert 250 - 5 \vert}{250} = \frac{245}{250} = 0.98
$$

**Step 3 — Check minimum threshold:** The recommended minimum Weber contrast for sustained readability is **0.5–0.7** (ISO 9241-303), and **0.8+** for safety-critical displays. Our 0.98 is well above threshold. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Heuristic:** If $C_{\text{Weber}} \geq 0.8$, the display passes the contrast criterion for **prolonged VDT work** in well-lit offices. If $C_{\text{Weber}} \geq 0.5$, it passes for **general office work**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Hick's Law for Choice Reaction Time

**Given:** A cockpit warning light panel has $N = 4$ indicator lamps. A pilot must identify and respond to whichever lamp illuminates.

**Step 1 — Hick's Law formula:**

$$
RT = a + b \log_2(N+1)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute typical values $a = 200$ ms, $b = 120$ ms/bit:**

$$
RT = 200 + 120 \log_2(4+1) = 200 + 120 \log_2(5)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\log_2(5)$:**

$$
\log_2(5) = \frac{\ln 5}{\ln 2} = \frac{1.6094}{0.6931} \approx 2.3219
$$

**Step 4 — Final RT:**

$$
RT = 200 + 120 \times 2.3219 = 200 + 278.6 \approx 478.6 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Implication:** A 4-lamp panel yields ≈ 480 ms RT. Doubling to 8 lamps (e.g., adding more status indicators) gives:

$$
RT_{8} = 200 + 120 \log_2(9) = 200 + 120 \times 3.1699 \approx 580.4 \text{ ms}
$$

That's ≈ **100 ms extra** — significant in a high-tempo cockpit.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Rule:** Group related controls/displays so the operator's *effective $N$* at any one decision point is ≤ 4 (Cowan). This obeys **Miller's Law** and keeps RT in the 400–500 ms range.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example: Fitts' Law for Pointer Movement

**Given:** A pilot moves a cursor from one icon to a target icon on a touchscreen. Distance between icons $D = 40$ mm; target icon width $W = 8$ mm.

**Step 1 — Compute Index of Difficulty (ID):**

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{D}{W}\right) = \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{40}{8}\right) = \log_2(6)
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate:**

$$
ID = \frac{\ln 6}{\ln 2} = \frac{1.7918}{0.6931} \approx 2.585 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Movement Time (using typical $a = 50$ ms, $b = 100$ ms/bit for touchscreen tapping):**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID = 50 + 100 \cdot 2.585 \approx 50 + 258.5 = 308.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Implication:** Larger target icons ($W \uparrow$) reduce $ID$ and thus $MT$. **Design heuristic:** keep target sizes ≥ **10–12 mm** on touchscreens viewed at arm's length to keep $MT < 300$ ms.

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table for Display Type

| Display Task | Hard Copy | Screen Text | Pictogram | Numeric Code | Best Practice |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Detailed reference (manual) | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★ | Paper manual, controlled lighting |
| Real-time status (10 indicators) | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | Screen, color + position coding |
| Safety warning (single, critical) | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★ | Red pictogram + redundant text |
| Quantitative trend (hours of data) | ★ | ★★★★★ | ★ | ★★ | Line chart on screen |
| Categorical comparison (≤ 5 cats) | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ | Bar chart on screen |
| Emergency action (immediate) | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★ | Pictogram + flashing light + auditory alarm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** This matrix is a high-yield synthesis. In ESE questions, you can reproduce an abbreviated version as a 7-mark answer to "Compare and contrast display media."

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Human Information Processing Pipeline (IPM)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Stimulus (Visual / Auditory / Tactile)"] --> node2["Sensory Register (Iconic / Echoic)"]
    node2 --> node3["Perception (Pattern Recognition)"]
    node3 --> node4["Working Memory (Short-Term)"]
    node5["Long-Term Memory (Schemas, Rules)"] <--> node4
    node4 --> node6["Decision Making (Hicks Law)"]
    node6 --> node7["Response Selection (Fitts Law)"]
    node7 --> node8["Motor Action (Execution)"]
    node8 --> node9["Response Output"]
    node9 --> node10["Feedback Loop to Perception"]

    classDef proc fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000

    class node3,node6,node7 proc
    class node4,node5 mem
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the *Model Human Processor* and list its three subsystems with their approximate cycle times.

> **Model Answer:**
> The **Model Human Processor (MHP)**, proposed by Card, Moran & Newell (1983), is a simplified computational model that decomposes human cognition into three interacting processors:
> 1. **Perceptual Processor** (handles sensory input) — cycle time $\tau_P \approx 100$ ms; decay ≈ 250 ms.
> 2. **Cognitive Processor** (handles memory and decisions) — cycle time $\tau_C \approx 70$ ms; decay ≈ 10–30 s.
> 3. **Motor Processor** (handles response output) — cycle time $\tau_M \approx 70$ ms; decay ≈ 200 ms.
>
> Each processor has its own memory store, and information flows sequentially P → C → M, although parallel processing is possible for independent tasks. The MHP forms the basis for predicting **reaction time, learning curves, and interface responsiveness** in HFE. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between **hard copy text** and **computer screen text** in terms of three human-factor design considerations.

> **Model Answer:**
> | Parameter | Hard Copy | Screen |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Luminance & Contrast** | Reflective (depends on ambient light); stable, glare-prone on glossy paper | Emissive (200–400 cd/m²); adjustable; susceptible to flicker and veiling glare |
> | **Resolution** | 300–2400 dpi (very fine strokes) | 72–218 ppi (limited by pixel pitch) |
> | **Viewing Distance & Stability** | 30–40 cm; image is perfectly stable | 50–70 cm; prone to flicker, jitter, and refresh artifacts |
>
> Hard copy excels in **resolution and stability**; screen text excels in **flexibility, searchability, and updateability**. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Explain the **Information Processing Model (IPM)** of the human operator with a neat block diagram. Discuss **Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory** and its design implications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Visual Acuity** and **Contrast Sensitivity**. A pilot must read a cockpit display at 750 mm with acuity 20/40. Calculate the **minimum character height** (in mm) for legibility, and recommend a **design height** with a 1.5× safety factor. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Question A (a)

The **Information Processing Model (IPM)** treats the human as an information channel analogous to a communication system. The classical stages are:

1. **Stimulus Reception:** Energy from the environment (light, sound, force) is captured by sensory receptors.
2. **Sensory Register (SR):** Briefly holds raw sensory data. Visual SR (iconic memory) lasts ≈ 250 ms; auditory SR (echoic) lasts ≈ 2 s.
3. **Perceptual Encoding:** Pattern-recognition processes convert raw input into meaningful objects.
4. **Working Memory (WM):** Active processing and short-term storage. Capacity ≈ 4 ± 1 chunks (Cowan, 2001), duration ≈ 20–30 s.
5. **Long-Term Memory (LTM):** Vast, semi-permanent store of schemas, skills, and facts.
6. **Decision Making:** Comparing perceived state with LTM to choose an action (Hick's Law).
7. **Response Selection & Execution:** Motor commands issued (Fitts' Law).
8. **Feedback:** Outcome perceived, closing the loop.

**Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory (MRT, 1984, 2002):** Wickens postulated that the human has *multiple, partially independent processing resources* categorized along three dimensions:

- **Processing Stages** (perceptual → cognitive → response)
- **Perceptual Modalities** (visual vs. auditory)
- **Visual Channels** (focal vs. ambient; spatial vs. verbal)
- **Response Channels** (manual vs. vocal)

**Design Implication:** Tasks using *different* resources can be performed in parallel with little interference; tasks competing for the *same* resource cause **dual-task interference** and performance degradation. Hence:
- Combine **auditory warnings** with **visual displays** (different modalities → parallel).
- Avoid showing **two spatial maps** on the same focal-vision screen.
- Use **voice input** alongside **manual control** (different response channels).

(7 marks: 4 marks for IPM diagram + explanation, 3 marks for MRT and design implications.)

---

##### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Visual Acuity (VA):** The angular resolution of the eye — the smallest angular detail (MAR, minimum angle of resolution) an observer can discriminate, typically expressed as a Snellen ratio or LogMAR.

**Contrast Sensitivity (CS):** The reciprocal of the minimum contrast (Weber or Michelson) required to detect a target. The **Contrast Sensitivity Function (CSF)** plots CS vs. spatial frequency, peaking at 2–5 cyc/deg.

**Calculation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Show the Snellen-to-MAR conversion explicitly; examiners reward numerical clarity.

**Step 1 — Patient MAR from 20/40:**

$$
\text{VA ratio} = \frac{20}{40} = 0.5 = 10^{\text{LogMAR}}
\Rightarrow \text{LogMAR} = \log_{10}(0.5) = -0.301
$$

Wait — LogMAR convention: $\text{LogMAR} = \log_{10}(d_{\text{test}}/d_{\text{ref}})$. For 20/40, $d_{\text{test}} = 20$, $d_{\text{ref}} = 40$:

$$
\text{LogMAR} = \log_{10}(20/40) = \log_{10}(0.5) = -0.301
$$

Conventionally, MAR is reported as positive (worse-than-normal is +ve LogMAR). 20/40 = LogMAR 0.301 (using inverse: 20/40 = 40/20 in LogMAR convention $\log_{10}(40/20) = 0.301$).

**Step 2 — Patient MAR in arc-minutes:**

$$
\text{MAR}_{\text{patient}} = 1' \times 10^{0.301} = 1' \times 2 = 2 \text{ arc-minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Stroke width at 750 mm:**

$$
s = d \cdot \tan(2') = 750 \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{10800}\right) = 750 \cdot 5.82 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.436 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Character height (5 strokes):**

$$
H = 5s = 5 \times 0.436 \approx 2.18 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply 1.5× safety factor:**

$$
H_{\text{design}} = 1.5 \times 2.18 \approx 3.27 \text{ mm}
$$

**Recommendation:** Use characters at least **3.3 mm tall** at 750 mm viewing distance for a 20/40 pilot, with a **stroke width of 0.65 mm** and a **Weber contrast ≥ 0.8**. (7 marks: 2 marks each for VA and CS definitions, 3 marks for calculation, 2 marks for recommendation.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** Describe the **Process of Seeing** with a labeled diagram of the eye. Explain the role of **rods and cones** in vision. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Accommodation**. A 50-year-old operator has a near point of 50 cm and a far point at infinity. Calculate the **amplitude of accommodation**. Comment on the design implications for an industrial inspection task. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution — Question B (a)

The **Process of Seeing** is the sequence by which visible light (380–780 nm) is captured, focused, transduced, and interpreted as visual perception.

**Anatomical Stages** (with diagram as in §4.2):

1. **Cornea:** Transparent front layer providing ≈ 43 D of refractive power.
2. **Aqueous Humor:** Clear fluid filling the anterior chamber, maintaining intraocular pressure.
3. **Pupil & Iris:** Variable aperture (2–7 mm) controlling retinal illuminance via the pupillary light reflex.
4. **Lens:** Biconvex, variable-power element (15–20 D at rest) that fine-tunes focus via accommodation.
5. **Vitreous Humor:** Gel-filled posterior chamber maintaining retinal shape.
6. **Retina:** Innermost photosensitive layer; contains:
   - **Rods** (≈ 120 million): Highly sensitive, **scotopic** (dim light), monochromatic, distributed in the peripheral retina. Peak spectral sensitivity ≈ 507 nm.
   - **Cones** (≈ 6 million): Less sensitive, **photopic** (bright light), trichromatic (S = 420 nm blue, M = 530 nm green, L = 560 nm red), concentrated in the **fovea** (≈ 2° visual angle).
7. **Optic Nerve & Lateral Geniculate Nucleus:** Carry signals to the visual cortex.
8. **Visual Cortex (V1, V2, V3, V4, V5):** Hierarchical processing — V1 detects edges/orientations, V4 processes color, V5 (MT) processes motion.

**Role of Rods vs. Cones:**

| Property | Rods | Cones |
|---|---|---|
| Quantity | ≈ 120 M | ≈ 6 M |
| Location | Peripheral retina | Fovea (concentrated) |
| Sensitivity | High (scotopic) | Low (photopic) |
| Color vision | None (achromatic) | Trichromatic (S, M, L) |
| Spatial acuity | Low | High |
| Temporal resolution | Slow | Fast |
| Light condition | Night / dim | Day / bright |

**Design Implication:** Lighting design must support both. Inspections of color-coded labels require **photopic (cone) lighting** (≥ 100 lux) to enable color discrimination. Night-time aviation displays need **scotopic-friendly** red or NVIS-compatible lighting to preserve dark adaptation. (7 marks: 4 marks for stages, 2 marks for rods/cones comparison, 1 mark for design implication.)

---

##### Model Solution — Question B (b)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Accommodation* is the ability of the eye's lens to change optical power to focus on objects at varying distances, achieved by contraction/relaxation of the **ciliary muscle** which alters lens curvature via the **zonular fibers**.

**Calculation of Amplitude of Accommodation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{\text{near}} &= 50 \text{ cm} = 0.50 \text{ m} \\
u_{\text{far}} &= \infty \text{ m} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{u_{\text{far}}} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
A = \frac{1}{u_{\text{near}}} - \frac{1}{u_{\text{far}}} = \frac{1}{0.50} - 0 = 2.0 \text{ D}
$$

**Comment on Duane's Table:** A 50-year-old's mean amplitude is ≈ **2.0 D** — exactly the calculated value, confirming **age-appropriate accommodation** (early presbyopia).

**Design Implications for Industrial Inspection:**

1. **Provide a magnified image or a longer working distance** to reduce accommodative demand. A 50 cm inspection distance is at the *very edge* of this operator's near point.
2. **Use a binocular stereo microscope** with adjustable dioptre settings to compensate.
3. **Increase ambient task lighting** (≥ 1000 lux for inspection) to reduce pupil constriction and increase depth of focus, effectively extending the near point.
4. **Allow task rotation** or **use progressive (multifocal) eyewear** so the operator can alternate between near and far tasks without straining.
5. **Ensure a clear zone of clear vision** (40–70 cm) for display monitoring; use **stand-mounted magnifiers** (e.g., 2×) to halve the accommodative demand.

(7 marks: 2 marks for definition, 2 marks for calculation, 3 marks for design implications.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Module:**
> 1. **Confusing Snellen and LogMAR notations:** 20/40 is *worse* than 20/20 — LogMAR is **positive** when acuity is worse. Always double-check.
> 2. **Forgetting units in accommodation formula:** $u$ must be in *metres* for the result to be in *dioptres*. Using cm gives a 100× error.
> 3. **Mixing Weber and Michelson contrasts:** Weber uses background luminance; Michelson uses max + min. Mixing them in the same problem = full-mark loss on a sub-part.
> 4. **Omitting the safety factor in character-height calculations:** Real designs use a 1.3–1.5× safety margin. Skipping it = –1 to –2 marks.
> 5. **Listing rods/cones but not contrasting them quantitatively:** Examiners reward *quantitative* comparison (counts, wavelengths, acuity). A purely qualitative list is half-credit at best.
> 6. **Drawing an IPM diagram without labeling all stages and arrows:** The diagram alone is worth only 1–2 marks; the explanation is the rest.
> 7. **Ignoring cultural neutrality in symbol design:** A 7-mark question on symbol design that omits ISO 9186 / cross-cultural testing is incomplete.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — High-Density Revision Points:**

- **HMS Model:** Human + Machine + Environment, optimized for **compatibility** and **match** between human capabilities and machine demands.
- **IPM Stages:** Stimulus → Sensory Register → Perception → WM → Decision → Response → Motor → Feedback. Miller/Cowan capacity ≈ **4 ± 1 chunks**.
- **MHP:** Perceptual $\tau \approx 100$ ms, Cognitive $\tau \approx 70$ ms, Motor $\tau \approx 70$ ms.
- **Hick's Law:** $RT = a + b \log_2(N+1)$; keep $N \leq 4$ to stay under 500 ms.
- **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \log_2(1 + D/W)$; larger targets and shorter distances = faster.
- **Process of Seeing:** Cornea (43 D) + Lens (15–20 D, variable) + Pupil (2–7 mm) + Retina (rods peripheral scotopic; cones foveal photopic, S/M/L trichromatic).
- **Accommodation:** $A = 1/u_{\text{near}} - 1/u_{\text{far}}$ in dioptres; **decreases with age** (Duane's curve); presbyopia = $A < 3$ D.
- **Visual Acuity:** Snellen ratio $d_{\text{test}}/d_{\text{ref}}$; MAR ≈ 1' (20/20); 5' for whole-letter legibility.
- **Contrast Sensitivity:** $CS = 1/C_T$; Weber $C = \Delta L / L$; Michelson $C = (L_{\max} - L_{\min})/(L_{\max} + L_{\min})$; CSF band-pass peaking at 2–5 cyc/deg.
- **Hard Copy vs Screen:** Paper has high resolution, no flicker, depends on ambient light; screen has flexibility, lower resolution, flicker + glare risk (CVS).
- **Min char height:** $H \geq 5 \cdot d \cdot \tan(\text{MAR})$ with **1.3–1.5× safety factor**.
- **Stroke width:** **8–12 %** of character height; **width-to-height ratio 0.7–0.9**.
- **VDT viewing distance:** **50–70 cm**; document holder at same distance, adjacent to screen.
- **Graphic Representations:** Tufte's data-ink ratio; Cleveland-McGill ranking (position best, color worst).
- **Symbol Design:** ISO 9186 / ISO 7001; ≥ **85 % comprehension**; pictograms + redundant coding for color-vision-deficient users.
- **Color Codes (ISO 3864 / ANSI Z535):** Red = danger/prohibition, Yellow = caution, Green = safety/proceed, Blue = mandatory action.
- **CVS Prevention:** **20-20-20 rule**; top of screen at or below eye level; ≥ 0.8 Weber contrast; anti-glare measures.
- **Design Heuristics to Memorize:** Keep $N \leq 4$, char height $\geq$ MAR-derived, contrast $\geq 0.8$ (prolonged) or $\geq 0.5$ (general), pictograms + text + color redundancy for safety-critical signs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visual Displays - Qualitative Visual Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Visual Displays - Qualitative Visual Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Qualitative Visual Display** is a human-machine interface element that conveys information regarding the **state, condition, mode, or status** of a system or process using **non-numerical, symbolic, or categorical cues**. Unlike quantitative displays (which present exact numerical magnitudes), qualitative displays communicate *whether something is on/off, safe/unsafe, normal/abnormal, present/absent*, or *which category* a value falls into.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEBT832, Module 2)**, qualitative visual displays are classified as a subset of *static and dynamic visual display devices* used in operator-machine interfaces, control rooms, cockpits, and consumer products. They rely on the human perceptual channels of **shape recognition, color discrimination, and spatial position coding**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction for Examiners:**
> - **Qualitative Display** → Conveys *what* or *which state* (categorical).
> - **Quantitative Display** → Conveys *how much* or *how many* (numerical magnitude).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine driving a car on a highway. The dashboard contains two very different types of displays:

1. The **speedometer needle** pointing at 80 km/h → This is a **quantitative display** (it gives you a precise numerical magnitude).
2. The **red oil-pressure warning light** glowing on the dashboard → This is a **qualitative display** (it tells you *the state* of the system: "Pressure is dangerously low!").

You do **not** need to read a number from the oil light; the **color (red)**, **shape (oil can icon)**, and **position (centered on the dash)** all work together to communicate the *condition* instantly, even in your peripheral vision while driving. This is the power of qualitative displays — **rapid categorical recognition under high workload conditions**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following engineering and human-factors metrics govern the design of qualitative visual displays:

- **Visual Angle (θ)** — measured in **arcminutes (′)**, must be ≥ **15 arcminutes (25′ preferred)** for legibility.
- **Luminance Contrast Ratio** — minimum **3:1** for general visibility, **7:1** recommended for critical warning signals.
- **Color Wavelength Bands** — Red ≈ **620–750 nm**, Green ≈ **495–570 nm**, Yellow ≈ **570–590 nm** (per CIE 1931 standards).
- **Response Latency (operator reaction)** — must be < **1 second** for hazard-indicating qualitative lights.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Qualitative displays are part of the broader "Visual Displays" module and are typically taught alongside quantitative displays, auditory displays, and tactile displays to build a complete understanding of multi-modal display design in human factors engineering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Qualitative Visual Displays

Qualitative displays can be systematically classified into **five principal categories** based on the perceptual cue they exploit:

1. **Indicator Lights (Signal Lamps)**
   - Discrete point-sources of illumination.
   - Used to denote *ON/OFF*, *GO/NO-GO*, *SAFE/UNSAFE*, or *READY/NOT READY* states.
   - Examples: Power-on LED on a computer, runway lights, alarm beacons.

2. **Flag Displays (Mechanical Flags)**
   - Physical mechanical vanes that flip or rotate to expose a colored face.
   - Provide *latching* qualitative information (state persists without power).
   - Examples: Aircraft landing-gear position indicators, railway signal flags.

3. **Color-Coded Scales and Zones**
   - Continuous scales (e.g., thermometers) partitioned into *colored bands* (green = safe, yellow = caution, red = danger).
   - The operator reads the *zone* (category) rather than the *exact value*.

4. **Symbolic / Pictorial Displays**
   - Use of internationally recognized icons (e.g., ⚠ for danger, 🔋 for battery).
   - Language-independent; reduce cognitive translation time.
   - Examples: ISO 7010 safety signs, dashboard warning pictograms.

5. **Status Panels and Grouped Indicators**
   - Arrays of lights, panels, or screens that collectively show the *system state* (e.g., overhead panel of an aircraft cockpit).

### 2.2 Design Principles — The "Why" and "How"

The design of qualitative displays is governed by five interlocking principles, each tied to human perceptual limits:

- **Principle of Pop-out (Visual Salience):** A critical qualitative cue must be *immediately detectable* in the user's field of view, even peripherally. Achieved via high luminance contrast, distinctive color, and unique shape.
- **Principle of Compatibility:** The display's meaning should match the operator's pre-existing mental model (e.g., red = stop/danger, green = go/safe). This is **population stereotype compatibility**.
- **Principle of Redundancy:** Convey the same qualitative information through *two independent perceptual channels* (e.g., color + flashing + audible beep) to ensure comprehension under degraded conditions.
- **Principle of Discriminability:** Two distinct states must never be confusable. E.g., green and red are highly discriminable; green and blue may be confused by 8% of males (color vision deficiency).
- **Principle of Interpretability:** The symbol/color/position must have a learned or universally understood meaning — ambiguity causes hesitation and error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these matter (Engineering Utility):** In safety-critical systems such as nuclear control rooms, surgical theaters, and aviation cockpits, qualitative displays are preferred for **alarm states** because they require *near-zero cognitive effort* to interpret, freeing the operator's working memory for diagnostic reasoning.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula or Rule | Units / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Minimum Visual Angle for Legibility | $\theta \geq 15'$ (absolute), $\theta \geq 25'$ (preferred) | arcminutes (′) |
| 2 | Visual Angle Computation | $\theta = \dfrac{2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2D}\right) \cdot 60}{1^\circ}$ (in arcmin) | $h$ = character height, $D$ = viewing distance |
| 3 | Luminance Contrast Ratio | $C = \dfrac{L_{\text{background}} - L_{\text{foreground}}}{L_{\text{background}}}$ | Dimensionless; min $\vert C \vert \geq 0.5$ |
| 4 | Minimum Contrast Ratio (Engineering) | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}} \geq 3{:}1$ general, $\geq 7{:}1$ critical | Ratio (no units) |
| 5 | Color Wavelength Bands (CIE) | Red $\approx 620$–$750\ \text{nm}$, Yellow $\approx 570$–$590\ \text{nm}$, Green $\approx 495$–$570\ \text{nm}$ | Nanometers (nm) |
| 6 | Reaction Time to Visual Warning | $t_r \approx 0.25$–$0.50\ \text{s}$ for salient stimuli | Seconds (s) |
| 7 | Snellen Visual Acuity Reference | 20/20 $\Leftrightarrow$ $\theta_{\min} = 1'$ of arc | arcminutes |
| 8 | Signal Detection Theory Criterion | $d' = \dfrac{\mu_s - \mu_n}{\sigma}$ | Standardized score |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes used in the table above.** All notation uses $\vert$ or $\mid$ within LaTeX delimiters to preserve markdown table integrity — this is critical for clean rendering in GitHub, Obsidian, and PDF converters used by KTU students.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Qualitative displays are indispensable in:

- **Aviation:** Master Caution/Warning lights (red = immediate action, amber = caution).
- **Medical Devices:** Defibrillator status LEDs, infusion pump alarms.
- **Industrial Control Rooms (SCADA/DCS):** Trip indicators, valve open/closed lights.
- **Automotive:** Engine check, airbag, seatbelt, fuel-level indicator lights.
- **Railways and Maritime:** Signal lights, status panels on bridge consoles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Character Height for Legible Qualitative Symbols

To design a qualitative symbol (e.g., a warning pictogram) that is **legible** at a given viewing distance, the engineer must satisfy the human visual acuity limit.

**Given:**
- Snellen visual acuity: 20/20 → minimum resolvable angle $\theta_{\min} = 1\ \text{arcminute}$.
- Recommended legibility safety factor: $k = 5$ (so $\theta = 5 \times 1' = 5'$ minimum, but KTU standard recommends 15').

**Step 1 — Write the visual angle formula:**

$$
\theta = \frac{2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{2D}\right) \cdot \dfrac{180}{\pi} \cdot 60}{1}
$$

Here, $\theta$ is in arcminutes, $h$ is the character/symbol height, and $D$ is the viewing distance.

**Step 2 — Use the small-angle approximation** (valid for $\theta < 10°$):

$$
\theta_{\text{arcmin}} \approx \frac{h \cdot 3438}{D}
$$

The constant $3438 = 60 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ converts radians to arcminutes and accounts for the $2$ factor.

**Step 3 — Solve for the required height $h$:**

$$
h = \frac{\theta \cdot D}{3438}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):**

Suppose a control-room operator views a panel from $D = 2000\ \text{mm}$ and the designer requires $\theta = 15\ \text{arcminutes}$ for legibility.

$$
h = \frac{15 \times 2000}{3438} = 8.72\ \text{mm}
$$

So the minimum pictogram height should be $\approx 8.7\ \text{mm}$. The preferred $\theta = 25'$ gives:

$$
h = \frac{25 \times 2000}{3438} = 14.54\ \text{mm}
$$

The KTU-recommended character height is therefore $\geq 14.5\ \text{mm}$ at 2 m viewing distance.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Luminance Contrast Ratio for a Warning Indicator

**Problem:** An indicator light has a background luminance $L_b = 50\ \text{cd/m}^2$ and a foreground (active warning LED) luminance $L_f = 450\ \text{cd/m}^2$. Verify if it meets the KTU critical-warning contrast criterion.

**Step 1 — Compute the Weber contrast:**

$$
C = \frac{L_f - L_b}{L_b} = \frac{450 - 50}{50} = 8.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the contrast ratio:**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{L_f}{L_b} = \frac{450}{50} = 9{:}1
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with standards:**

- General minimum: $3{:}1$ → satisfied ✓
- Critical-warning minimum: $7{:}1$ → satisfied ✓ (achieved $9{:}1$)

**Conclusion:** This indicator meets both general and critical-warning contrast standards and is safe to deploy.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Display Type by Information Need

| Information Need | Recommended Qualitative Display | Perceptual Cue | Risk if Misused |
|---|---|---|---|
| System ON/OFF | Indicator light (LED) | Color + position | Operator misses "OFF" = false sense of de-energization |
| Danger / Hazard | Flashing red light + pictogram | Color + flash + shape | Degraded reaction if color-blindness not considered |
| Mode selection (e.g., AUTO/MANUAL) | Illuminated pushbutton or flag | Color + label | Mode confusion → automation surprise |
| Status overview (multi-system) | Status panel (grouped LEDs) | Spatial grouping | Visual clutter → inattentional blindness |
| Categorical magnitude (low/med/high) | Color-banded scale | Color + position | Operator over-precise interpretation |

### 3.4 Signal Detection Theory Applied to Qualitative Displays

For a qualitative alarm, the designer must minimize **misses** (failing to see the warning) and **false alarms** (false positives). The sensitivity index $d'$ is:

$$
d' = \frac{\mu_s - \mu_n}{\sigma}
$$

where $\mu_s$ and $\mu_n$ are the means of the "signal+noise" and "noise-only" internal-response distributions, and $\sigma$ is their common standard deviation. A well-designed qualitative warning maximizes $d'$ by increasing the perceptual distance between the signal and the background (high contrast, distinctive color, unique shape).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Taxonomy of Qualitative Visual Displays

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["Qualitative Visual Displays"]
    rootA --> catA["Indicator Lights"]
    rootA --> catB["Flag Displays"]
    rootA --> catC["Color Coded Zones"]
    rootA --> catD["Symbolic Pictorial"]
    rootA --> catE["Status Panels"]

    catA --> exA1["Power ON LED"]
    catA --> exA2["Alarm Beacon"]
    catA --> exA3["Dashboard Warning"]

    catB --> exB1["Landing Gear Flag"]
    catB --> exB2["Railway Signal Flag"]

    catC --> exC1["Thermometer Zones"]
    catC --> exC2["Pressure Gauge Bands"]

    catD --> exD1["ISO 7010 Safety Signs"]
    catD --> exD2["Dashboard Pictograms"]

    catE --> exE1["Aircraft Overhead Panel"]
    catE --> exE2["SCADA Mimic Panel"]

    style rootA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style catA fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style catB fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style catC fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style catD fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
    style catE fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Design Process Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1["Step 1 Identify Information Need"]
    step2["Step 2 Select Display Category"]
    step3["Step 3 Apply Color Coding Standard"]
    step4["Step 4 Compute Visual Angle"]
    step5["Step 5 Verify Contrast Ratio"]
    step6["Step 6 User Test for Discriminability"]
    step7["Step 7 Deploy in System"]

    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7

    style step1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style step2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style step3 fill:#E0FFFF
    style step4 fill:#E0FFFF
    style step5 fill:#E0FFFF
    style step6 fill:#F0FFF0
    style step7 fill:#90EE90
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Operator Interaction with Qualitative Displays

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcA["Physical System Sensor"]
    srcB["Process Variable e.g. Temperature"]

    srcA --> proc1["Signal Conditioning and Threshold Logic"]
    srcB --> proc1

    proc1 --> dispA["Indicator Light ON OFF"]
    proc1 --> dispB["Color Band Scale Green Yellow Red"]
    proc1 --> dispC["Pictogram Warning Icon"]
    proc1 --> dispD["Auditory Backup Beep"]

    dispA --> eyeA["Operator Visual Channel"]
    dispB --> eyeA
    dispC --> eyeA
    dispD --> earA["Operator Auditory Channel"]

    eyeA --> brainA["Perceptual Interpretation Categorical State"]
    earA --> brainA
    brainA --> actionA["Operator Decision and Action"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Notes:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `rootA`, `catA`, `step1`, `srcA`, `dispA`) and never use reserved Mermaid keywords as standalone names. Node labels contain plain uppercase alphanumeric text only — no bold, italics, or HTML tags — to guarantee successful rendering in all Mermaid versions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*Define a qualitative visual display. How does it differ from a quantitative visual display? Give two examples of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **qualitative visual display** is a visual interface element that conveys the *state, condition, or category* of a system using non-numerical cues such as color, shape, position, or symbol, rather than precise numerical magnitude. A **quantitative visual display**, in contrast, communicates the *exact value* of a variable, allowing the operator to read off numerical magnitudes.

| Qualitative | Quantitative |
|---|---|
| Red engine warning light (state: fault present) | Speedometer needle at 80 km/h (magnitude) |
| Green "Power ON" LED (state: energized) | Digital multimeter reading 230 V (magnitude) |

The key distinction is **categorical versus numerical** information representation. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*List and briefly explain any three design principles that govern the effective use of qualitative visual displays.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Principle of Pop-out (Visual Salience):** Critical qualitative cues must be instantly detectable in the field of view via high contrast, distinctive color, and unique shape. (1 mark)
2. **Principle of Population Stereotype Compatibility:** Colors and symbols should match universally accepted conventions (e.g., red = danger, green = safe). (1 mark)
3. **Principle of Redundancy:** The same qualitative information should be conveyed via two independent channels (e.g., color + flashing + audible alarm) to ensure comprehension under degraded conditions such as bright sunlight or color-vision deficiency. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Types, Design, and Engineering Utility*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 marks]** *Classify the different types of qualitative visual displays used in human-machine systems. With suitable examples, explain indicator lights, flag displays, and symbolic/pictorial displays.*

**Model Answer:**

Qualitative visual displays are classified into five principal categories:

1. **Indicator Lights (Signal Lamps):** Discrete point-source illuminations (typically LEDs) that signal a binary or categorical state. *Example:* The red "battery" pictogram-light on a car dashboard indicates a charging-system fault. Advantages include instant pop-out, low power consumption, and compatibility with population stereotypes (red = warning).

2. **Flag Displays:** Mechanical vanes or plates that physically rotate or flip to expose a colored surface. They provide *latching* qualitative state indication — the state persists even if power is lost. *Example:* The landing-gear "down-and-locked" indicators in older aircraft cockpits; railway signal flags. They are highly reliable in high-vibration or EMI-prone environments.

3. **Symbolic / Pictorial Displays:** Use of internationally standardized icons (e.g., ISO 7010) that convey qualitative information independent of language. *Example:* The triangular ⚠ warning sign, the pictogram of a fuel pump at a refueling station, the seatbelt reminder icon. They reduce cognitive translation time and are accessible to operators with low literacy or non-native language background.

4. **Color-Coded Scales and Zones** *(brief mention):* Continuous instruments whose scale is partitioned into colored bands. *Example:* A mercury thermometer with green/yellow/red zones.

5. **Status Panels** *(brief mention):* Arrays of grouped indicators conveying system-wide state. *Example:* Aircraft overhead status panel. (7 marks distributed across the three asked categories plus brief context)

---

**(b) [7 marks]** *An indicator lamp in an industrial control room must be legible at a viewing distance of 2.5 m. Calculate the minimum diameter of the lamp's illuminated symbol. Assume the KTU standard visual angle of 15 arcminutes for legibility.*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Viewing distance $D = 2.5\ \text{m} = 2500\ \text{mm}$
- Required visual angle $\theta = 15\ \text{arcminutes}$

**Step 1 — Apply the visual angle formula:**

$$
h = \frac{\theta \cdot D}{3438}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
h = \frac{15 \times 2500}{3438} = \frac{37500}{3438} = 10.91\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — State the result:**

The minimum symbol diameter should be approximately **10.91 mm**. To be safe, the designer should adopt the KTU preferred visual angle of 25 arcminutes, which yields:

$$
h_{\text{preferred}} = \frac{25 \times 2500}{3438} = 18.18\ \text{mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** A lamp symbol of at least $\approx 11\ \text{mm}$ diameter (preferably $18\ \text{mm}$) is recommended. (7 marks: 2 marks for formula, 3 marks for substitution and calculation, 1 mark for the preferred-value check, 1 mark for engineering conclusion)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 marks]** *Explain the role of color coding in qualitative visual displays. Discuss the population-stereotype color conventions and the limitations imposed by color-vision deficiency (CVD).*

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Color Coding:** Color is the most powerful single perceptual cue in qualitative displays because it is processed in pre-attentive vision — the operator perceives the color *before* conscious attention is engaged. This enables near-instant recognition of system state and supports detection even in peripheral vision.

**Population-Stereotype Conventions (ANSI Z535 / ISO 3864):**
- **Red** → Danger, immediate hazard, stop, fire, emergency stop.
- **Yellow / Amber** → Caution, warning, potential hazard.
- **Green** → Safety, normal operation, go.
- **Blue** → Mandatory action, information.
- **White / Grey** → Neutral, no specific meaning.

**Limitations due to Color-Vision Deficiency (CVD):**
Approximately **8% of males and 0.5% of females** have some form of CVD (red-green deficiency being most common). Pure color coding can therefore fail for a non-trivial fraction of operators.

**Engineering Mitigations:**
1. **Redundant coding:** Pair color with shape, position, or label (e.g., a triangular ⚠ shape, not just yellow).
2. **Avoid problematic color pairs:** Do not rely solely on red-vs-green discrimination for critical signals.
3. **Use luminance contrast** in addition to chromatic contrast, since CVD primarily affects hue discrimination, not luminance.
4. **Use blue + yellow or blue + white** combinations for high CVD-safety.

(7 marks: 2 marks role, 2 marks conventions, 2 marks CVD statistics and types, 1 mark mitigations)

---

**(b) [7 marks]** *A control-room alarm panel has a background luminance of $80\ \text{cd/m}^2$. The active warning indicator has a luminance of $640\ \text{cd/m}^2$. Determine (i) the Weber contrast, (ii) the contrast ratio, and (iii) verify whether the indicator meets the KTU critical-warning criterion.*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $L_b = 80\ \text{cd/m}^2$
- $L_f = 640\ \text{cd/m}^2$

**Part (i) — Weber Contrast:**

$$
C = \frac{L_f - L_b}{L_b} = \frac{640 - 80}{80} = \frac{560}{80} = 7.0
$$

**Part (ii) — Contrast Ratio:**

$$
\text{CR} = \frac{L_f}{L_b} = \frac{640}{80} = 8{:}1
$$

**Part (iii) — Verification against KTU Standards:**

- General minimum: $\text{CR} \geq 3{:}1$ → Achieved $8{:}1$ ✓
- Critical-warning minimum: $\text{CR} \geq 7{:}1$ → Achieved $8{:}1$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The indicator satisfies both the general and critical-warning luminance contrast standards and is approved for deployment as a critical alarm. (7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 distributed across the three sub-parts)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Qualitative Display Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing qualitative with quantitative displays** — students often describe a speedometer under "qualitative displays," losing 1–2 marks instantly. Always emphasize *categorical/non-numerical* for qualitative and *numerical magnitude* for quantitative.
> 2. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners specifically deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units like arcminutes, mm, cd/m², or nm.
> 3. **Ignoring color-vision deficiency** — any color-coding question MUST mention CVD; failing to do so is a recurrent 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Skipping the small-angle approximation justification** — when using the visual-angle formula, state that it is valid for $\theta < 10°$.
> 5. **Forgetting the safety factor** — always state both the absolute minimum (15′) and preferred (25′) visual angle in design problems.
> 6. **Not providing the engineering conclusion** — KTU expects a closing sentence such as "Hence, the display is safe / not safe to deploy" — omitting it costs the last mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Qualitative visual displays convey *state/category*, not numerical magnitude.
- **Five main types:** Indicator lights, flag displays, color-coded zones, symbolic/pictorial, and status panels.
- **Key design principles:** Pop-out, compatibility, redundancy, discriminability, interpretability.
- **Visual angle standard:** $\theta \geq 15'$ absolute, $\theta \geq 25'$ preferred.
- **Visual angle formula:** $h = \dfrac{\theta \cdot D}{3438}$, with $h$ in mm and $D$ in mm.
- **Contrast standards:** $\text{CR} \geq 3{:}1$ (general), $\text{CR} \geq 7{:}1$ (critical warning).
- **Weber contrast:** $C = \dfrac{L_f - L_b}{L_b}$.
- **Color stereotypes (ISO 3864 / ANSI Z535):** Red = danger, Yellow = caution, Green = safe, Blue = mandatory.
- **CVD consideration:** ~8% of males are color-vision deficient; use redundant coding.
- **Reaction time:** Salient visual warnings trigger response in 0.25–0.50 s.
- **Engineering domains:** Aviation, medical, SCADA, automotive, railway, maritime.
- **Always state units, safety factor, and engineering conclusion** in KTU numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quantitative Visual Displays
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Quantitative Visual Displays

# Quantitative Visual Displays — A Comprehensive Engineering Ergonomics Study Guide

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Quantitative Visual Display** is a human-machine interface element engineered to communicate precise numerical information (magnitude, value, or quantity of a variable) to an operator through the visual channel. According to KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEBT832) syllabus, quantitative displays are classified as a sub-category of *Visual Displays* within the broader taxonomy of human-factor display instrumentation, distinct from *Qualitative Displays* (which only indicate direction/trend) and *Check-reading Displays* (which verify if a value is within limits).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Quantitative displays are designed primarily for **reading** tasks where the user must extract a specific numerical value of a measured variable. The accuracy, precision, and legibility of the read-out directly depend on the ergonomic design parameters of the display.

The formal hierarchical classification of visual displays as per the standard Human Factors Engineering (HFE) framework taught at KTU is:

$$\text{Visual Displays} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Qualitative Displays (direction only)} \\ \text{Quantitative Displays (numerical magnitude)} \\ \text{Check-reading Displays (limit verification)} \end{cases}$$

Further sub-classification of Quantitative Displays:

$$\text{Quantitative Displays} \rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Digital (Numeric) Displays} \\ \text{Analog Displays} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The unit explicitly covers dial design, pointer characteristics, scale graduations, moving-scale vs. moving-pointer conventions, and the **Wickens/Goldman** design recommendations for quantitative readouts.

### 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy — "The Speedometer Story"

Imagine you are driving a car and want to know exactly how fast you are going.

- **Scenario A (Digital Display):** The dashboard shows "**87 km/h**" as glowing digits. You can read this with a *single glance* — the precision is absolute (no interpretation), and you don't need to align anything. This is great for **precise reading** but poor for **tracking trends** (e.g., is the speed rising or falling?).

- **Scenario B (Analog Dial):** The needle points to a position between "**80**" and "**90**". You must mentally interpolate ("about 85"). However, your peripheral vision can detect whether the needle is moving **up (accelerating)** or **down (decelerating)** — even if you don't focus on it. This is excellent for **trend monitoring**.

- **Scenario C (Counter Display):** An odometer showing "**45,378 km**" — this is a quantitative display used for **cumulative check-reading**, where the absolute value matters but rate-of-change is irrelevant.

This car dashboard is essentially a **microcosm of all quantitative visual display problems** — and the rest of this study guide analyzes every engineering design parameter that goes into making such displays optimally readable.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Metrics & Standard Units in Display Ergonomics

| Ergonomic Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit / Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Visual Angle | $\theta$ | **arc-minutes (′)** | Threshold for legibility |
| Preferred Display Luminance | $L_d$ | **35–100 cd/m²** | Avoid display darkening |
| Recommended Pointer-to-Scale Gap | $g_p$ | **1.6–4.8 mm** | Prevents parallax error |
| Pointer Width | $W_p$ | **1/10 of smallest scale division** | Optimal legibility |
| Scale Graduation Height | $H_g$ | **≥ 2.5 mm** at viewing distance | Minimum readable size |
| Decimal Point Width | $W_{dp}$ | **1.5 × digit stroke width** | Readability standard |
| Preferred Viewing Distance | $d_v$ | **≈ 71 cm (28 inches)** | Reference office distance |
| Snellen Visual Acuity Reference | VA | **6/6 (20/20)** | 1 arc-minute resolution |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Primary Tasks Served by Quantitative Displays

Every quantitative display in any engineering system (cockpit, control panel, medical monitor, CNC machine) is designed to support at least one of the following cognitive tasks:

1. **Read a value** (e.g., altitude = 5,200 ft)
2. **Track a trend** (e.g., temperature is *rising*)
3. **Set a value** (e.g., dial-in a setpoint of 75 °C)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Critical Design Rule:** The display's *physical configuration* (analog vs. digital, fixed vs. moving scale) must be matched to the *dominant task*. A common KTU examination pitfall is recommending a digital display for a *tracking* task — this is ergonomically wrong.

### 2.2 Detailed Taxonomy of Quantitative Displays

#### (A) Digital (Numeric) Displays
These present values as discrete numerals — LED, LCD, seven-segment, or dot-matrix readouts.

**Engineering Advantages:**
- Absolute precision (no interpolation error)
- Faster reading for specific values
- Compact (low spatial requirement)

**Engineering Disadvantages:**
- Poor trend detection (requires sequential reading)
- Power consumption (especially LED)
- Higher cognitive load for change detection

**Sub-types of Numeric Displays:**
- **Purely numeric** (e.g., 00472) — used for counters
- **Decimal numeric** (e.g., 47.2) — preferred when fractional accuracy is needed
- **Multiple-scale numeric** (e.g., 00472 km / 293 mi) — switchable units

#### (B) Analog Displays
These use a *spatial position* (pointer angle, bar height, or column level) to encode a magnitude.

**Engineering Advantages:**
- Superior for trend/rate perception
- Natural mapping (left-to-right, bottom-to-top = increasing)
- Peripheral-vision readable

**Engineering Disadvantages:**
- Requires interpolation → reading error (typically $\pm 0.5$ smallest scale division)
- Larger panel space requirement
- Subject to parallax

**Sub-types of Analog Displays:**
- **Moving-Pointer / Fixed-Scale** (e.g., classic speedometer) — most common
- **Moving-Scale / Fixed-Pointer** (e.g., clock face, radio tuning dial) — used where reading is rarely required but reference is fixed
- **Counter-Pointer** (e.g., aircraft Mach meter with rotating drum numerals)
- **Bar/Column displays** (e.g., audio level meters)

### 2.3 Critical Design Variables — The Engineering Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> These are the **board-exam essential** parameters. A KTU question often asks: *"List the design factors for an analog dial display"* or *"Explain the pointer-to-scale relationship."*

#### (i) Dial Scale Characteristics

| Parameter | KTU Recommended Value | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
