# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define a "signal" in the context of human factors engineering. Differentiate between a *signal* and a *warning light* with one engineering example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **signal** in human factors engineering is a pre-arranged, codified form of energy (visual, auditory, or tactile) emitted by a machine or system to communicate operational status, condition, or required response to a human operator. Signals are general-purpose information carriers and may convey routine, advisory, or critical information.

A **warning light** is a specific category of visual signal designed to capture immediate operator attention and indicate the presence of a hazardous or abnormal condition that requires prompt corrective action. Warning lights are characterized by high luminance contrast, distinctive color (typically red), and a flashing pattern in the 4–6 Hz range.

**Example — Signal:** The steady green "Power On" LED on a laboratory instrument. It conveys routine, continuous information (the system is energized) without urgency.

**Example — Warning Light:** The flashing red "Master Warning" annunciator on an aircraft glareshield. It indicates an immediate flight-critical condition (e.g., engine fire, low oil pressure) requiring pilot action within seconds.

**Key Distinction:** All warning lights are signals, but not all signals are warning lights. The defining property of a warning light is its **safety-critical urgency**, encoded through high contrast, distinctive color, and rapid flashing.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: List and briefly explain any three design parameters that govern the effectiveness of a warning light.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Luminance Contrast Ratio ($C$):** The ratio of the signal's luminance to the background luminance, expressed as $C = (L_s - L_b) / L_b$. A minimum $C \geq 0.5$ is required for general signals, and $C \geq 0.8$ is recommended for safety-critical warning lights. Without adequate contrast, the warning may be masked by ambient illumination.

2. **Flash Rate ($f$):** The frequency at which the light cycles between on and off states. For caution signals, $f$ should be 0.5–1 Hz (slow, advisory). For warning lights, $f$ should be 4–6 Hz (fast, attention-capturing). Flash rates must remain below the Critical Flicker Fusion (CFF) threshold (~15–60 Hz depending on ambient light) to retain the attention-getting motion effect.

3. **Color Coding:** The selection of a specific wavelength (e.g., red = danger, yellow = caution, green = safe) per ISO 3864. Color is the fastest pre-attentive visual feature, processed in 20–50 ms, making it the most efficient channel for encoding semantic urgency.

*(Additional acceptable parameters: size/visual angle, signal-to-noise ratio, redundancy with auditory/haptic signals, viewing angle of the operator, ambient lighting conditions.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry 14 marks and offer internal choice within the module. The model solutions below provide exhaustive, step-by-step answers.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical warning light system used in an industrial process control environment. Discuss the role of each block in ensuring reliable operator alerting.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A control room has a background luminance of $L_b = 80 \text{ cd/m}^2$ due to overhead lighting. A red warning lamp with a luminance of $L_s = 300 \text{ cd/m}^2$ is to be installed. Calculate: (i) the Weber contrast ratio, (ii) the visual signal-to-noise ratio in dB, and (iii) determine whether the lamp meets the recommended warning threshold of $C \geq 0.8$ and $VSNR \geq 20$ dB.

#### Model Solution for (a):

The functional architecture of a warning light system in a process control environment comprises the following blocks, shown in the **Diagram 2** of Section 4.2 of these notes:

1. **Process/Sensor Block:** The physical process (e.g., a chemical reactor, a steam boiler) is monitored by sensors (temperature, pressure, level transmitters) that convert physical quantities into proportional electrical signals (typically 4–20 mA current loops in industrial practice).

2. **Signal Conditioning and Threshold Detection Block:** The raw sensor signals are amplified, filtered, and compared against pre-set safety thresholds. If a threshold is exceeded, a trip signal is generated. This block embodies the safety logic (e.g., IEC 61508 SIL-rated functions).

3. **Controller/Microprocessor Block:** A PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) or DCS (Distributed Control System) executes the safety logic and determines which alarm class to activate (advisory, caution, warning, or emergency). It selects the appropriate color, flash rate, and redundant output channels.

4. **Output Driver Block:** Power electronics (relays, solid-state switches, LED drivers) modulate the warning lamp with the correct voltage, current, and flashing pattern. The flash rate is typically generated by a 555-timer circuit or microcontroller PWM output.

5. **Warning Lamp Module Block:** The physical light-emitting element (incandescent, halogen, LED, or Xenon strobe), often with a colored filter or colored LED (red for danger, yellow for caution, green for safe). The lamp is sized to provide adequate luminance and viewing angle.

6. **Operator Perception Block:** The visual signal is transmitted to the operator's retina, processed by the lateral geniculate nucleus and visual cortex, and interpreted semantically. Effective design exploits pre-attentive features (color, motion) to minimize cognitive load.

7. **Auditory/Haptic Redundancy Block:** In parallel with the visual warning, an audible alarm (buzzer, siren, or voice message) and/or haptic signal (control chair vibration) is activated. This redundancy follows the **Defense in Depth** principle: if one channel fails (e.g., operator looking away, hearing protection in use), another channel conveys the alert.

8. **Feedback and Annunciation Block:** The operator takes corrective action (e.g., closing a valve, activating a fire suppression system). The system then either clears the alarm (if the condition is resolved) or escalates to higher-priority alerts.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Background luminance: $L_b = 80 \text{ cd/m}^2$
- Signal luminance: $L_s = 300 \text{ cd/m}^2$
- Required: $C \geq 0.8$, $VSNR \geq 20$ dB

**(i) Weber Contrast Ratio:**

$$
C = \dfrac{L_s - L_b}{L_b}
$$
$$
C = \dfrac{300 - 80}{80}
$$
$$
C = \dfrac{220}{80}
$$
$$
C = 2.75
$$

> **Result:** $C = 2.75$. Since $2.75 \geq 0.8$, the lamp **exceeds** the warning-level contrast requirement by a factor of ~3.4.

**(ii) Visual Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dB):**

$$
VSNR_{dB} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{L_s}{L_b}\right)
$$
$$
VSNR_{dB} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{300}{80}\right)
$$
$$
VSNR_{dB} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}(3.75)
$$
$$
VSNR_{dB} = 20 \times 0.574 = 11.48 \text{ dB}
$$

> **Result:** $VSNR \approx 11.48$ dB.

**(iii) Compliance Check:**

- **Contrast Check:** $C = 2.75 \geq 0.8$ → **PASS** (exceeds threshold by significant margin).
- **SNR Check:** $VSNR = 11.48 \text{ dB} < 20 \text{ dB}$ → **FAIL** (does NOT meet the 20 dB reliability threshold).

**Engineering Conclusion and Recommendation:** The lamp has **excellent contrast** against the background and is therefore visually distinguishable. However, the signal-to-noise ratio of 11.48 dB is **below** the recommended 20 dB threshold for safety-critical warnings. This indicates that, although the lamp is brighter than the background, the *relative* margin is insufficient when accounting for visual clutter, reflections, and operator variability.

**Recommended Action:** Either (a) install a higher-luminance lamp such that $L_s \geq 800 \text{ cd/m}^2$ (yielding $VSNR = 20 \cdot \log_{10}(800/80) = 20 \text{ dB}$ exactly), or (b) reduce the ambient background luminance by using anti-glare panel coatings and lower-intensity overhead lights. Option (b) is generally preferred as it reduces operator visual fatigue.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Alternative Question]

**Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Critical Flicker Fusion (CFF)** and its significance in the design of warning lights. How does the choice of flash rate interact with the CFF threshold, and what design rule should an engineer follow to ensure that a warning light remains effective?

**(b) [7 Marks]** An operator views a warning indicator from a distance of $D = 1.8$ m. The indicator is a circular LED lamp of diameter $12$ mm. Calculate: (i) the visual angle subtended at the eye in arc-minutes, (ii) the minimum diameter that would still allow reliable detection (assuming the 1-arc-minute Snellen threshold), and (iii) comment on whether the actual indicator is appropriately sized for a safety-critical warning application.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Definition of Critical Flicker Fusion (CFF):**

Critical Flicker Fusion is the frequency at which a flashing light source ceases to be perceived as intermittent (flickering) and instead appears as a **steady, continuous** illumination. This perceptual transition occurs because the human visual system cannot resolve temporal luminance changes faster than the CFF threshold.

The CFF depends primarily on:
- **Retinal illuminance:** Higher ambient light raises the CFF. In photopic (daylight) conditions, CFF ≈ 50–60 Hz. In scotopic (dim) conditions, CFF can drop to 15–20 Hz.
- **Luminance of the source:** Brighter lamps increase CFF.
- **Retinal location:** Foveal (central) vision has higher CFF than peripheral vision.
- **Age and fatigue:** CFF decreases with age and operator fatigue.

**Significance for Warning Light Design:**

The design objective is to select a flash rate that is:
- **Fast enough** to engage the pre-attentive motion-detection system (which requires motion, not steady illumination).
- **Slow enough** to remain *below* the CFF threshold, so the lamp continues to *appear* to flash rather than fuse into apparent steadiness.

If the flash rate exceeds CFF, the warning light will look like a **steady lamp** to the operator, and the entire attention-getting benefit of flashing is lost.

**Design Rule:**

For a warning light designed to operate under typical indoor industrial lighting (~200–500 lux), the CFF is approximately 40–50 Hz. The engineer must therefore select a flash rate **comfortably below** this, with margin for:

- Higher-than-expected ambient illumination.
- Age-related CFF reduction in operators.
- Lamp-to-lamp variation in driver circuits.

The recommended flash rate is therefore **4 to 10 Hz**, with the **4–6 Hz sub-band** being the "sweet spot" for maximum attention capture. A typical engineering rule is:

$$
f_{design} \approx \dfrac{1}{2} \times f_{CFF,\,expected}
$$

For $f_{CFF} = 40$ Hz: $f_{design} \approx 20$ Hz maximum, but practically constrained to ≤10 Hz to avoid operator stress.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Viewing distance: $D = 1.8$ m
- LED diameter: $h = 12$ mm $= 0.012$ m
- Snellen threshold: $\theta_{min} = 1$ arc-minute

**(i) Visual Angle Subtended:**

$$
\theta = \dfrac{h}{D} = \dfrac{0.012}{1.8} = 0.00667 \text{ rad}
$$

Converting to degrees:

$$
\theta_{deg} = 0.00667 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 0.00667 \times 57.296 = 0.382^\circ
$$

Converting to arc-minutes:

$$
\theta_{arcmin} = 0.382 \times 60 = 22.92 \text{ arc-minutes}
$$

> **Result:** $\theta \approx 22.92$ arc-minutes (or ~23 arc-minutes).

**(ii) Minimum Diameter for Reliable Detection (1 arc-minute):**

$$
h_{min} = D \times \theta_{min} \text{ (in radians)}
$$

Converting 1 arc-minute to radians:

$$
1 \text{ arc-minute} = \dfrac{\pi}{10800} \text{ rad} \approx 2.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}
$$

$$
h_{min} = 1.8 \times 2.909 \times 10^{-4} = 5.236 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.524 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Result:** $h_{min} \approx 0.52$ mm.

**(iii) Sizing Comment:**

The actual LED diameter is **12 mm**, which is **~23 times larger** than the minimum detectable size of 0.52 mm. The indicator is therefore **grossly over-sized** for mere detection, but this is **appropriate and desirable** for a safety-critical warning light, because:

- Larger physical size increases the **salience index** and improves peripheral detection (when the operator is not looking directly at the panel).
- Oversizing provides a **safety margin** against:
  - Partial obscuration (dust, fingerprints, scratches on the lens).
  - Off-axis viewing angles.
  - Reduced visual acuity in operators wearing prescription eyewear or with age-related decline.
- For warning-level applications, the **target visual angle** is typically **5–15 arc-minutes**, not the bare 1 arc-minute threshold. The actual 23 arc-minutes falls at the **upper end** of this recommended range.

> **Conclusion:** The 12 mm LED is **appropriately sized** for a safety-critical warning application. It exceeds the bare detection threshold by a factor of ~23, comfortably meeting the warning-level salience requirements.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** A common student error in this type of question is to **omit the unit conversion** between radians, degrees, and arc-minutes. The 1 arc-minute threshold is a non-SI unit, and the conversion factor is $1^\circ = 60$ arc-minutes and $180^\circ = \pi$ rad. Students who compute $h_{min}$ directly in radians without converting to arc-minutes for the comparison will lose **2 marks** out of the 7 allocated. Additionally, do **not** present the formula $h = 0.0022 \times D$ in Part (b) without derivation — examiners want to see the unit conversion explicitly, especially for design-style problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following high-density checklist is your rapid-revision summary for the topic "Signals & Warning Lights" (Module 2, OEEBT832). Review this list in the final 30 minutes before the examination.

### Core Definitions
- **Signal:** A codified form of energy (visual/auditory/tactile) conveying operational information from machine to human.
- **Warning Light:** A safety-critical visual signal demanding immediate operator action.
- **Pre-attentive Feature:** A visual attribute (color, motion, luminance change) processed in <200 ms without serial attention.
- **CFF (Critical Flicker Fusion):** Frequency above which flashing appears steady (~15–60 Hz depending on luminance).
- **Defense in Depth:** Safety principle of using redundant parallel alert channels (visual + auditory + haptic).

### The Four-Tier Urgency Hierarchy
1. **Indicator:** Steady green/white, $f = 0$ Hz, low intensity → "Normal"
2. **Caution:** Yellow/amber, slow flash $f = 0.5$–$2$ Hz, moderate intensity → "Attention"
3. **Warning:** Red, fast flash $f = 4$–$6$ Hz, high intensity → "Act Now"
4. **Emergency:** Red strobe, $f > 6$ Hz or strobing, maximum intensity + audible → "Life Threat"

### Must-Memorize Formulas
- **Luminance Contrast:** $C = (L_s - L_b) / L_b \geq 0.8$ for warnings.
- **Snellen / Min Size:** $h_{min} = 0.00022 \times D$ (meters).
- **Visual Angle:** $\theta = h / D$ (small-angle approximation, rad).
- **Weber's JND:** $\Delta I / I = k$, with $k \approx 0.08$ for luminance.
- **VSNR:** $VSNR_{dB} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}(L_s / L_{noise}) \geq 20$ dB for critical.
- **Color Convention:** Red = danger, Yellow = caution, Green = safe, Blue = mandatory (per ISO 3864 / ANSI Z535).

### Standards to Reference in Exam Answers
- **ISO 3864-1:2011** — Safety colours and safety signs.
- **ANSI Z535.1-2017** — Safety color coding (US).
- **IEC 60601-1-8** — Medical alarm signals.
- **MIL-STD-1472** — Human engineering (defense applications).
- **FAA AC 25.1381-2** — Aircraft warning lights.

### Common Pitfalls to Avoid
1. **Forgetting to specify flash rate** along with color and intensity — always give all three.
2. **Using generic "red light"** without specifying luminance, contrast, and viewing distance.
3. **Confusing indicator and warning light** — they are NOT interchangeable in safety-critical applications.
4. **Designing a single-modality alert** — always consider auditory/haptic redundancy.
5. **Ignoring the CFF** — a flash rate >60 Hz looks steady and defeats the purpose.
6. **Forgetting unit conversions** (radians ↔ degrees ↔ arc-minutes).
7. **Ignoring ambient conditions** — a lamp sized for an indoor control room is not necessarily adequate for outdoor daylight.

### High-Yield Memory Hooks
- **"Red Means Stop, Yellow Means Slow, Green Means Go"** — the universal traffic-light mnemonic extends to all industrial signaling per ISO 3864.
- **"4 Hz is the magic number"** — the optimal warning flash rate, derived from psychophysical research on human pre-attentive motion detection.
- **"Contrast is King"** — color and flash are useless without adequate luminance contrast ($C \geq 0.8$).
- **"Defense in Depth"** — never rely on a single sensory channel for safety-critical warnings.

---

**End of Module 2 — Topic: Signals & Warning Lights | OEEBT832 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Representational Displays - Aircraft Bank Angle Displays; Principles of Aircraft Position Displays; Auditory Displays - Process of Hearing; Principles of Auditory Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Representational Displays - Aircraft Bank Angle Displays; Principles of Aircraft Position Displays; Auditory Displays - Process of Hearing; Principles of Auditory Displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Representational Displays — Foundational Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** A *Representational Display* is a display element in which a physical quantity or system state is depicted through a spatial, geometric, or pictorial configuration that bears a direct analogical correspondence to the variable being represented. The mapping between the symbol/pointer and the underlying variable preserves the *dimensionality*, *direction*, and *metaphor* of the controlled variable.

**Two Architectural Sub-classes (Sanders & McCormick, 1993 framework adopted by KTU):**

| Sub-class | Mapping Nature | Human Processing |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct (Pictorial / Contact-Analog) Display** | The display element literally resembles the controlled variable (e.g., aircraft symbol moving over a horizon line) | Pattern recognition, perceptual constancy — minimal cognitive translation |
| **Indirect (Symbolic / Abstract) Display** | A pointer moves across a calibrated scale (e.g., needle on a calibrated bank gauge) | Reading, value-extraction, mental arithmetic — high cognitive load |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Dashboard Clock vs. Compass"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you are walking. A **dashboard clock** showing your heading in degrees is an *indirect display* — your brain must translate "needle pointing to 90°" → "I am facing East." A **physical compass strapped to your chest**, where the needle physically aligns with Earth's magnetic field, is a *direct display* — the symbol IS the reality. The human visual system evolved to read direct displays almost effortlessly; indirect displays require training. This is the central design tension in aircraft instrumentation.

### 1.3 Aircraft Bank Angle Displays — Definitions and Scope

**Bank Angle** is the rotation of an aircraft about its longitudinal (nose-to-tail) axis, measured in degrees from the wings-level reference. Standard instrument convention treats **0° = wings level, +90° = right wing down, −90° = left wing down**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Attitude Indicator (AI):** The primary flight instrument that simultaneously displays bank (roll) angle AND pitch (nose-up/nose-down) angle relative to the true horizon. It is the canonical "Earth-referenced direct display" in modern aviation.

**Three Historical Generations of Bank Angle Displays:**

1. **Mechanical (Indirect) — The Turn-and-Slip / Inclinometer**  
   A small ball floats inside a curved glass tube (the "coordinator"). Ball position infers rate of turn via physics, not via direct pictorial correspondence. Bank is read from a *separate* needle gauge.

2. **Gyroscopic Pictorial (Contact-Analog) — The "Miniature Aircraft" or "Attitude Indicator"**  
   A miniature airplane symbol stays fixed; an artificial horizon (driven by a vertical gyroscope) moves behind it. **This is direct representation** — the pilot sees a "world view."

3. **Glass Cockpit / Primary Flight Display (PFD) — Synthetic Video Display**  
   CRT/LCD screen renders the horizon, pitch ladder, and bank index digitally. Conceptually identical to the gyroscopic AI but adds synthetic overlays (flight director bars, heading bugs).

### 1.4 Aircraft Position Displays — Scope and Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Module 2):** *Aircraft Position Displays* refer to the family of instruments conveying the aircraft's spatial state in three rotational degrees of freedom (pitch, roll, yaw) and three translational degrees of freedom (latitude, longitude, altitude). They are governed by the principle of *Spatial Compatibility* — the movement of the display element must be congruent with the expected movement of the controlled variable.

**Canonical Examples:**
- **Attitude Indicator (AI)** — pitch + roll (the "six-pack" workhorse)
- **Heading Indicator (HI) / Directional Gyro (DG)** — yaw, magnetic reference
- **Altimeter** — vertical translation (altitude)
- **Horizontal Situation Indicator (HSI)** — composite of heading + course + bearing
- **Radar/EFIS moving map** — horizontal translation (lat/long)

### 1.5 Auditory Displays — Core Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An *Auditory Display* is a human-machine interface in which information is conveyed to the user through controlled acoustic signals — variations in frequency, intensity, temporal pattern, or spatial location — leveraging the human auditory system as the sensing channel.

**Typical Use Cases (per KTU 2024 Module 2):**
- Aircraft cockpit warning tones (Master Caution, Stick Shaker, Terrain Awareness)
- Sonar/radar operator signal detection
- Medical equipment alarms (ventilators, IV pumps)
- Vehicle reverse-warning beeps
- Telephone dial tones, DTMF signalling

### 1.6 The Process of Hearing — Foundational Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Hearing:** *Hearing* is the perceptual and physiological process by which airborne (or water-borne) pressure fluctuations are transduced by the ear into neural impulses that the central nervous system interprets as pitch, loudness, and timbre.

**Three Anatomical Stages (must be memorized verbatim for KTU 2024):**

| Stage | Anatomical Substrate | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Outer Ear** | Pinna, External Auditory Canal, Tympanic Membrane (Eardrum) | Collects sound waves, funnels them to the eardrum, impedance matching begins |
| **Middle Ear** | Three Ossicles — *Malleus (Hammer), Incus (Anvil), Stapes (Stirrup)* — and the Oval Window | Mechanical impedance matching and amplification (~22×) via the **Ossicular Lever Ratio** and the **Areal Ratio** |
| **Inner Ear (Cochlea)** | Cochlear duct, Basilar Membrane, Organ of Corti, Hair Cells (inner & outer), Auditory (VIIIth Cranial) Nerve | Transduces mechanical vibration into neural spike trains via the *shearing action* of hair-cell stereocilia |

**Two Neural Pathways (post-cochlea):**
- **Auditory Nerve (VIIIth)** → Cochlear Nuclei → Superior Olivary Complex → **Lateral Lemniscus** → Inferior Colliculus → **Medial Geniculate Nucleus (MGB)** of Thalamus → Primary Auditory Cortex (Heschl's Gyrus, Brodmann Areas 41/42)

### 1.7 Intuitive Analogy — The Hearing as "Mechanical Microphone"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of the ear as a **3-stage analog signal-processing microphone**:  
> - **Outer ear** = the *microphone casing* (collects sound)  
> - **Middle ear** = the *transformer & pre-amp* (boosts a weak air-pressure signal ~22× so it can drive the dense cochlear fluid without losing energy)  
> - **Inner ear** = the *frequency-analyzing DSP chip* (the basilar membrane acts as a real-time **Fourier analyzer** — different positions along the membrane resonate to different frequencies, a phenomenon called **tonotopy**)  
> - **Auditory nerve** = the *digital output cable* to the CPU (brain)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Representational Displays — The Six Design Heuristics

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Governing Principles (KTU 2024 Module 2, Section on Display Design):**

1. **Principle of Pictorial Realism** — The display should look like the variable it represents. *(e.g., artificial horizon looks like the real horizon.)*
2. **Principle of the Moving Part** — If a variable is conceptually static and the rest of the system is conceptually moving, the moving element should represent the *moving* part of the system. *(e.g., in a moving-map display, the aircraft symbol stays centered while the map scrolls — the moving element is the map, not the aircraft.)*
3. **Principle of Perceptual Compatibility** — The mapping between display movement and controlled-variable movement must align with the operator's natural expectations.
4. **Principle of Spatial Compatibility** — Display element movement should be *geometrically parallel* to the system response. *(e.g., moving the yoke RIGHT → aircraft banks RIGHT → artificial horizon tilts RIGHT.)*
5. **Principle of Functional Compatibility** — Group related controls and displays by *function*, not by *hardware proximity*.
6. **Principle of Population Stereotype** — Where a strong population-wide convention exists (e.g., up = more, right = more), the display should follow it.

### 2.2 Aircraft Bank Angle Displays — Design Taxonomy and Tradeoffs

| Design Type | Pictorial Realism | Reading Speed | Error Rate | Training Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Indirect — Numeric (e.g., "BANK 23°" on glass readout) | **None** | Slow (must read & interpret) | **High** | Low |
| Indirect — Pointer on Scale (mechanical dial) | Low | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate |
| Direct (Pictorial) — Miniature Aircraft + Horizon | **High** | Fast (perceptual) | **Low** | High |
| Synthetic PFD (Head-Up Display variant) | Very High | Fastest | Lowest | Highest |

### 2.3 Principles of Aircraft Position Displays — Detailed Engineering Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Design Criteria (from KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus):**

**(a) Direction-of-Motion Compatibility:** The displacement of the display index on the instrument face must match the direction of the airplane's movement in the *operator's external frame of reference*. For example, when the aircraft **noses up**, the horizon line on the attitude indicator moves **down** (because the airplane symbol stays fixed in the display housing — this preserves "fixed reference frame" perception).

**(b) Frame of Reference:** Three valid choices exist — *aircraft-fixed* (pilot feels), *earth-fixed* (artificial horizon style), or *rotating* (compass card). Earth-fixed is preferred for *position*; aircraft-fixed for *rate* indicators.

**(c) Scale Linearity and Range:** Bank-angle display range is typically **±60° to ±90°** with linear scaling. Non-linear scaling violates Weber's Law expectations.

**(d) Resolution and Quantization:** The minimum increment that the pilot can *reliably discriminate* — for a bank display, this is approximately **2°** under good viewing conditions.

**(e) Redundancy and Cross-Check:** No single instrument is trusted — pilots use the AI + Turn Coordinator + Standby Instruments cross-check methodology.

### 2.4 The Process of Hearing — Step-by-Step Engineering of Sound Perception

**Step 1 — Sound Wave Capture (Outer Ear):**
- Sound Pressure Level (SPL) is funneled through the pinna, which adds **HRTF (Head-Related Transfer Function)** cues for vertical localization.
- Sound waves strike the **tympanic membrane** causing it to vibrate.

**Step 2 — Mechanical Amplification (Middle Ear):**
- The three ossicles form a class-1 lever. Combined with the **areal ratio** of tympanic membrane (~55 mm²) to oval window (~3.2 mm²) the pressure amplification is ~17×, lever ratio adds ~1.3× → total ~22× pressure gain.

**Step 3 — Impedance Matching:**
- Air impedance $Z_{air} \approx 413 \, \text{N·s/m}^3$ vs. cochlear fluid impedance $Z_{fluid} \approx 1.5 \times 10^5 \, \text{N·s/m}^3$.
- Without middle-ear amplification, **~99.9% (≈30 dB)** of acoustic energy would reflect. Middle ear recovers ~30 dB.

**Step 4 — Frequency Analysis (Cochlea):**
- The basilar membrane runs ~35 mm long, narrow at the base (responds to high frequencies) and wide at the apex (responds to low frequencies).
- The **Place Theory of Hearing (Békésy)** establishes that a pure tone of frequency $f$ produces a maximum displacement peak at a specific position $x(f)$ along the basilar membrane.

**Step 5 — Neural Transduction:**
- Inner hair cells (~3500) and outer hair cells (~12000) in the Organ of Corti deflect their **stereocilia**, opening ion channels and generating receptor potentials.
- Action potentials travel along **Type I** (95% of fibers, high-SR, encode fine temporal structure) and **Type II** (5%, low-SR) afferent fibers of the **vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII)**.

**Step 6 — Central Processing:**
- Superior Olivary Complex: first binaural integration site → **Interaural Time Difference (ITD)** for low-frequency localization (< 1500 Hz) and **Interaural Level Difference (ILD)** for high-frequency localization (> 3000 Hz).
- Auditory Cortex: sound recognition, pattern matching, speech decoding (Wernicke's area in left hemisphere).

### 2.5 Principles of Auditory Displays — Engineering Design Rules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Design Principles for Auditory Displays (KTU 2024 Module 2):**

1. **Detectability Principle** — The auditory signal must be **10–15 dB above the ambient noise floor** at the operator's ear for reliable detection.
2. **Discriminability Principle** — Different signal meanings must be encoded in *orthogonal* auditory dimensions (frequency, intensity, temporal pattern, spatial location). Operators can reliably discriminate **~5 distinct acoustic signals** (Miller's "magical number seven minus two" applied to auditory short-term memory).
3. **Compatibility Principle** — The acoustic property should map intuitively to the message. Example: *higher pitch = higher urgency* (Stevens' "psychophysical law of intensity"). *Faster pulse rate = greater urgency*.
4. **Standardization Principle** — Where cross-cultural / cross-application conventions exist (e.g., **ISO 7731** for danger signals, **ICAO Annex 14** for airport beacons, **IEC 60601-1-8** for medical alarms), designers MUST follow them.
5. **Redundancy / Multiple Coding Principle** — Pair an auditory alarm with a visual alarm whenever the operator's visual channel may be loaded (e.g., cockpit Master Caution = aural *BEEP* + amber *MASTER CAUTION* caption).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table — No Pipe-Conflicts)

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Numerical Value / Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sound Pressure Level | $L_p$ | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | $p_{\text{ref}} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ (RMS); unit = **dB SPL** | Auditory display intensity calibration |
| Sound Intensity Level | $L_I$ | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right)$ | $I_{\text{ref}} = 10^{-12} \,\text{W/m}^2$ | Inverse-square law, distance attenuation |
| Inverse-Square Law | $I(r)$ | $I(r) = \dfrac{P}{4\pi r^{2}}$ | Power $P$ spreads over spherical area | Predicting intensity at operator's ear |
| Frequency Range of Human Hearing | $f$ | — | $f_{\min} \approx 20 \,\text{Hz}$ ; $f_{\max} \approx 20{,}000 \,\text{Hz}$ | Auditory display design frequency band |
| Voice Communication Band | $f_{\text{voice}}$ | — | $300 \,\text{Hz} \leq f \leq 3400 \,\text{Hz}$ | Telephone / radio channel design |
| Middle-Ear Pressure Gain | $G_{p}$ | $G_{p} = A_{\text{ratio}} \times L_{\text{ratio}}$ | $\approx 17 \times 1.3 \approx 22$ | Restores ~30 dB of acoustic energy lost to impedance mismatch |
| Areal Ratio (Middle Ear) | $R_{A}$ | $R_{A} = \dfrac{A_{\text{tympanic}}}{A_{\text{oval window}}}$ | $\approx \dfrac{55 \,\text{mm}^2}{3.2 \,\text{mm}^2} \approx 17$ | Mechanical amplification step |
| Ossicular Lever Ratio | $R_{L}$ | $R_{L} = \dfrac{l_{\text{handle of malleus}}}{l_{\text{long process of incus}}}$ | $\approx 1.3$ | Mechanical amplification step |
| Equal-Loudness Contour (Phon) | $L_{N}$ | $L_{N} = 40 + 10\!\left(2^{N-1} - 1\right) \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{f}{1000}\right)$ for $N \geq 1$ | Phon unit matches dB SPL only at 1 kHz | Auditory display perceived loudness, A-weighting |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR}$ | $\text{SNR} = L_{\text{signal}} - L_{\text{noise}}$ | Required: $\geq 10$ dB for intelligibility | Detectability principle verification |
| Reaction Time (Auditory) | $RT$ | $RT \approx 150 \text{ to } 300 \,\text{ms}$ | Faster than visual (~250–500 ms) | Justifies auditory alarms for time-critical events |
| Weber's Law (Auditory) | $\Delta I / I$ | $\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} = k \approx 0.10$ for intensity | "Just Noticeable Difference" (JND) | Display increment design (bank-angle JND ~ 2°) |
| Bank-Angle Range (AI) | $\theta$ | $-90^{\circ} \leq \theta \leq +90^{\circ}$ | Convention: right wing down = positive | Standard display range |
| Pitch-Attitude Range (AI) | $\phi$ | $-30^{\circ} \leq \phi \leq +50^{\circ}$ | Standard PFD envelope | Standard display range |
| Critical Bandwidth (Ear's Frequency Resolution) | $\text{CB}$ | $\text{CB} \approx 0.2 f$ for $f > 500 \,\text{Hz}$ | Bark scale (Zwicker) | Determines number of distinguishable auditory channels |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these formulas matter to a KTU 2024 engineer:**
> - **Aerospace / Avionics Design:** Knowing the $L_p$ equation lets a human-factors engineer verify that a stall warning at 4000 Hz is at least 10 dB above cockpit noise (typically ~75 dB SPL).
> - **Medical Device Safety (IEC 60601-1-8):** Hospital alarm loudness must be ≥ 10 dB above ambient OR ≥ 45 dB(A), whichever is higher.
> - **Automotive ADAS:** Inverse-square law determines how loud a backup beep must be at 1 m to remain detectable at 5 m.
> - **Architectural Acoustics:** Bank angle displays and auditory warnings must function across temperature and altitude — sound speed varies $\approx 0.6 \,\text{m/s per °C}$ and is **331 m/s at 0°C, 343 m/s at 20°C**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sound Pressure Level (SPL) from a Measured Pressure

**Problem Context (Sample 1):** A cockpit stall-warning siren produces a sound pressure of $p = 0.2 \, \text{Pa}$ (RMS) at the pilot's ear. The cockpit ambient noise is $p_n = 0.02 \, \text{Pa}$. Verify that the signal is detectable per the Detectability Principle.

**Given:**  
$p_{\text{signal}} = 0.2 \, \text{Pa}$  
$p_{\text{noise}} = 0.02 \, \text{Pa}$  
$p_{\text{ref}} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{Pa}$

**Step 1 — Compute the signal SPL:**

$$
L_{p,\text{signal}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{signal}}}{p_{\text{ref}}}\right)
$$

Substituting:

$$
L_{p,\text{signal}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the argument of the logarithm:**

$$
\frac{0.2}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.2}{2.0 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.2}{0.00002} = 10{,}000 = 10^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the logarithm:**

$$
L_{p,\text{signal}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{4}\right)
$$

$$
\log_{10}\!\left(10^{4}\right) = 4
$$

**Step 4 — Final value:**

$$
L_{p,\text{signal}} = 20 \times 4 = 80 \,\text{dB SPL}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the noise SPL (same procedure):**

$$
L_{p,\text{noise}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.02}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{3}\right) = 60 \,\text{dB SPL}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):**

$$
\text{SNR} = L_{p,\text{signal}} - L_{p,\text{noise}} = 80 - 60 = 20 \,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 7 — Detectability Verdict:**

$$
\text{SNR} = 20 \,\text{dB} \; \geq \; 10 \,\text{dB} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{SIGNAL IS DETECTABLE} \;\; \blacksquare
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inverse-Square Law and the Detectability Distance

**Problem Context (Sample 2):** A factory-floor emergency stop siren radiates $P = 0.5 \, \text{W}$ of acoustic power uniformly in all directions. An operator is standing $r_1 = 1 \,\text{m}$ from the siren. The ambient factory noise is $L_{p,n} = 75 \,\text{dB SPL}$. Compute the maximum distance $r_{\max}$ at which the siren remains detectable (i.e., its SPL is still at least 10 dB above ambient).

**Step 1 — Compute intensity at $r_1 = 1$ m:**

$$
I(r_1) = \frac{P}{4\pi r_1^{2}} = \frac{0.5}{4\pi (1)^{2}} = \frac{0.5}{12.566} = 0.0398 \,\text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to intensity level:**

$$
L_{I}(r_1) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{\text{ref}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.0398}{10^{-12}}\right)
$$

$$
\frac{0.0398}{10^{-12}} = 3.98 \times 10^{10}
$$

$$
L_{I}(r_1) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(3.98 \times 10^{10}\right) = 10\,(10 + \log_{10} 3.98) = 10\,(10 + 0.600) = 106.0 \,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the required intensity at the threshold distance (10 dB above ambient):**

$$
L_{I,\min} = L_{p,n} + 10 = 75 + 10 = 85 \,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert threshold intensity level back to linear intensity:**

$$
I_{\min} = I_{\text{ref}} \times 10^{L_{I,\min}/10} = 10^{-12} \times 10^{8.5} = 10^{-3.5} = 3.16 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Use the inverse-square law to find $r_{\max}$:**

$$
I(r) = \frac{P}{4\pi r^{2}} \;\Rightarrow\; r = \sqrt{\frac{P}{4\pi I(r)}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
r_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5}{4\pi \times 3.16 \times 10^{-4}}}
$$

$$
r_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{0.5}{3.97 \times 10^{-3}}} = \sqrt{125.94} = 11.22 \,\text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Answer:**

$$
r_{\max} \approx 11.2 \,\text{m} \;\; \blacksquare
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bank-Angle Indicator Resolution (Weber's Law Application)

**Problem Context (Sample 3):** A bank-angle indicator must satisfy Weber's Law for the typical operating range $0^{\circ} \leq \theta \leq 60^{\circ}$. The Weber fraction for visual angular discrimination is $k = 0.033$. Determine the minimum tick-mark spacing on the dial at full scale.

**Step 1 — Weber's Law Statement:**

$$
\Delta\theta = k \cdot \theta
$$

**Step 2 — Apply at full scale $\theta = 60^{\circ}$:**

$$
\Delta\theta = 0.033 \times 60 = 1.98^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply at small bank $\theta = 5^{\circ}$ (cruise):**

$$
\Delta\theta_{\min} = 0.033 \times 5 = 0.165^{\circ}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Decision:** Choose the larger of the two values to satisfy the law everywhere: $\Delta\theta = 1.98^{\circ}$. However, since pilots are most sensitive at small banks, the standard 5° tick spacing is a *practical* compromise.

**Step 5 — Final spacing decision:**

$$
\boxed{\Delta\theta_{\text{standard}} = 5^{\circ} \text{ (tick)} \; ; \; \Delta\theta_{\text{minor}} = 1^{\circ} \text{ (subdivision)}}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Middle-Ear Pressure Gain (Ossicular + Areal Ratio)

**Step 1 — Pressure gain from areal ratio:**

$$
G_{\text{area}} = \frac{A_{\text{tympanic}}}{A_{\text{oval}}} = \frac{55 \,\text{mm}^2}{3.2 \,\text{mm}^2} = 17.19
$$

In dB:

$$
20 \log_{10}(17.19) = 20 \times 1.235 = 24.7 \,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Pressure gain from lever ratio:**

$$
G_{\text{lever}} = 1.3 \;\Rightarrow\; 20 \log_{10}(1.3) = 2.28 \,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Total pressure gain:**

$$
G_{\text{total}} = G_{\text{area}} \times G_{\text{lever}} = 17.19 \times 1.3 = 22.35
$$

In dB:

$$
G_{\text{total,dB}} = 24.7 + 2.28 = 26.98 \,\text{dB} \approx 27 \,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Impedance loss without middle ear:**

The transmission coefficient $\tau$ from air to cochlear fluid is:

$$
\tau = \frac{4 Z_{\text{air}} Z_{\text{fluid}}}{\left(Z_{\text{fluid}} + Z_{\text{air}}\right)^{2}}
$$

Substituting $Z_{\text{air}} = 413$ and $Z_{\text{fluid}} = 1.5 \times 10^{5}$:

$$
\tau = \frac{4 (413)(1.5 \times 10^{5})}{(1.5 \times 10^{5} + 413)^{2}} = \frac{2.478 \times 10^{8}}{(1.500004 \times 10^{5})^{2}} = \frac{2.478 \times 10^{8}}{2.250 \times 10^{10}} = 0.0110
$$

**Step 5 — Power loss in dB without middle ear:**

$$
L_{\text{loss}} = 10 \log_{10}(\tau) = 10 \log_{10}(0.011) = -19.6 \,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 6 — Effective recovery (using 22× pressure gain gives 27 dB):**

The middle ear nearly perfectly compensates the ~20 dB loss. Remaining ~7 dB deficit is made up by the **active amplification** of the outer hair cells (cochlear amplifier, ~40–60 dB at low levels).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Display Type vs. Human Performance

| Display Type | Reading Speed (ms) | Error Rate (%) | Mental Workload (Cooper-Harper equiv.) | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pure Pictorial (AI) | 250–400 | < 2 | Low | Primary attitude control |
| Pictorial + Numeric (PFD) | 200–350 | < 1 | Very Low | Modern glass cockpit |
| Pointer on Scale (mechanical) | 500–900 | 5–10 | Medium | Legacy, training |
| Pure Numeric (digital readout) | 800–1500 | 10–25 | High | Backup / data-link |
| Auditory (verbal ATC) | 1500–2500 (decode) | 5–15 | High | Information, not control |
| Auditory (non-verbal alarm) | 150–300 (detect) | < 2 (detect) | Low | Warning, alerting |

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Auditory Display Parameter vs. Perceived Attribute

| Physical Stimulus Dimension | Perceptual Dimension | Use in Display | Engineering Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency $f$ (Hz) | Pitch (mels) | Status, urgency coding | 20 Hz – 20 kHz audible band |
| Intensity $I$ (W/m²) | Loudness (sones, phons) | Severity, distance | Pain threshold ~ 120 dB SPL |
| Temporal Pattern (pulses/sec) | Rhythm / Tempo | Urgency, priority | Max distinguishable ~ 5 patterns |
| Spectral Content (harmonics) | Timbre | Identity, source | Critical bands ~ 24 Bark |
| Spatial Origin (HRTF) | Apparent Location | Source identification | Localization accuracy ~ 1° in front, 15° behind |
| Duration (ms) | Apparent "Length" | Confirmation / cancellation | < 100 ms blends; > 500 ms perceived as separate event |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Display Design Taxonomy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[REPRESENTATIONAL DISPLAYS] --> B[DIRECT PICTORIAL]
    A --> C[INDIRECT SYMBOLIC]
    A --> D[COMBINED / GLASS COCKPIT]

    B --> B1[Artificial Horizon - AI]
    B --> B2[HSI - Heading]
    B --> B3[MFD Moving Map]

    C --> C1[Mechanical Dial Gauge]
    C --> C2[Digital Readout]
    C --> C3[Vintage Turn Coordinator]

    D --> D1[Primary Flight Display PFD]
    D --> D2[Engine Indicating and Crew Alerting System EICAS]
    D --> D3[Head-Up Display HUD]

    B1 --> B1a[Pictorial Realism HIGH]
    B1 --> B1b[Spatial Compatibility YES]
    C1 --> C1a[Pictorial Realism LOW]
    C1 --> C1b[Reading REQUIRED]

    D1 --> D1a[REDUNDANT CODING]
    D1 --> D1b[POPULATION STEREOTYPE FOLLOWED]
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Process of Hearing (Anatomical Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ACOUSTIC PRESSURE WAVE] --> B[OUTER EAR - PINNA AND CANAL]
    B --> C[TYMPANIC MEMBRANE VIBRATION]
    C --> D[MIDDLE EAR OSSICLES - MALLEUS INCUS STAPES]
    D --> E[OVAL WINDOW - IMPEDANCE MATCHED]
    E --> F[INNER EAR COCHLEA - BASILAR MEMBRANE]
    F --> G[HAIR CELL TRANSDUCTION - ORGAN OF CORTI]
    G --> H[AUDITORY NERVE - CN VIII]
    H --> I[COCHLEAR NUCLEI]
    I --> J[SUPERIOR OLIVARY COMPLEX - ITD AND ILD]
    J --> K[LATERAL LEMNISCUS]
    K --> L[INFERIOR COLLICULUS]
    L --> M[MEDIAL GENICULATE BODY - THALAMUS]
    M --> N[PRIMARY AUDITORY CORTEX - HESCHLS GYRUS]
    N --> O[PERCEPTION - PITCH LOUDNESS TIMBRE LOCATION]

    D --> D1[Amplification approximately 22x]
    F --> F1[Tonotopic Frequency Analysis]
    G --> G1[Stereocilia Deflection]
    J --> J1[Binaural Localization]
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Auditory Display Design Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NEW AUDITORY DISPLAY PROPOSAL] --> B{CRITICALITY LEVEL}
    B -->|HIGH - DANGER| C[USE MULTIPLE CHANNELS - AUDITORY PLUS VISUAL]
    B -->|MEDIUM - CAUTION| D[USE PULSED TONE]
    B -->|LOW - ADVISORY| E[USE BRIEF CHIME]

    C --> F[STANDARD TONE LIBRARY FOLLOWED]
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F --> G{FREQUENCY CHOICE}
    G -->|URGENT| H[HIGH FREQUENCY 2500 TO 4000 HZ]
    G -->|INFORMATIONAL| I[VOICE BAND 500 TO 2500 HZ]

    H --> J[INTENSITY CHECK]
    I --> J

    J --> K{Lp AT OPERATOR EAR}
    K -->|BELOW 10 DB ABOVE AMBIENT| L[REDESIGN - INCREASE AMPLITUDE]
    K -->|AT OR ABOVE 10 DB ABOVE AMBIENT| M[ACCEPT]

    M --> N[FINAL DISPLAY VALIDATED]
    L --> G
```

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Bank Angle Display Reading Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PILOT LOOKS AT BANK ANGLE SOURCE] --> B{INSTRUMENT TYPE}
    B -->|ATTITUDE INDICATOR| C[READ HORIZON TILT ANGLE]
    B -->|MECHANICAL DIAL| D[READ NEEDLE POSITION ON SCALE]
    B -->|PFD SYNTHETIC| E[READ BANK INDEX VS BANK POINTER]

    C --> F{INFER BANK DIRECTION}
    D --> F
    E --> F

    F -->|HORIZON TILTS RIGHT| G[RIGHT WING DOWN - POSITIVE BANK]
    F -->|HORIZON TILTS LEFT| H[LEFT WING DOWN - NEGATIVE BANK]

    G --> I[APPLY CONTROL INPUT IF REQUIRED]
    H --> I

    I --> J[CROSS CHECK WITH TURN COORDINATOR]
    J --> K[CONTINUE MONITORING]
```

### 4.5 Diagram 5 — Frequency-to-Pitch Mapping of the Cochlea (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[OVAL WINDOW - HIGH FREQUENCY END - BASE] --> B[APEX - LOW FREQUENCY END]
    B --> C[COCHLEA LENGTH 35 MM]

    A --> A1[20000 HZ - NARROW STIFF REGION]
    A1 --> A2[4000 HZ - SPEECH CONSONANTS]
    A2 --> A3[1000 HZ - SPEECH VOWELS]
    A3 --> B1[500 HZ]
    B1 --> B2[100 HZ]
    B2 --> C1[20 HZ - WIDE FLEXIBLE HELICOTREMA]
```
