# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Define the "Sliding Filament Theory" of muscle contraction. State TWO pieces of evidence that prove the filaments themselves do not shorten during contraction.**

**Model Answer:**
The Sliding Filament Theory (Huxley & Hanson, 1954) states that muscle shortening during contraction is produced by the **interdigitation** of pre-existing actin and myosin filaments, which *slide past one another* without themselves changing length.

**Evidence:**
1. **Constant A-band length:** Microscopy of contracting and resting sarcomeres shows the A-band (region of myosin) does not change in length, while the I-band and H-zone shrink.
2. **Constant filament mass:** X-ray diffraction patterns show that the spacing of actin and myosin monomers remains constant during contraction — only their overlap changes.

*(Examiner's note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for evidence = 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Style]**

**Differentiate between Type I and Type IIx skeletal muscle fibers in terms of (i) myosin ATPase activity, (ii) fatigue resistance, and (iii) typical ergonomic role.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Type I (Slow Oxidative) | Type IIx (Fast Glycolytic) |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------------------|
| (i) Myosin ATPase | Slow hydrolytic rate | Very high hydrolytic rate |
| (ii) Fatigue Resistance | Very High (aerobic, mitochondria-rich) | Very Low (anaerobic, glycogen-dependent) |
| (iii) Ergonomic Role | Postural maintenance, low-intensity sustained tasks | Burst exertion, emergency lifts, sprint actions |

*(Award 1 mark per correct row.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3]*

**(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, describe the **molecular mechanism of cross-bridge cycling** in skeletal muscle. Explain the role of ATP in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A warehouse worker is required to push a 35 kg trolley with a horizontal force of 80 N at a steady speed of 0.6 m/s on a flat surface. Calculate the **mechanical work done per minute**, the **average mechanical power output**, and the **metabolic power** assuming 20 % muscle efficiency. Comment on the work intensity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a)

1. **Diagram:** Show the four states of the cross-bridge cycle (Attachment, Power stroke, Detachment, Re-cocking) with arrows indicating ATP and Pi exchange. *(2 marks)*
2. **Attachment:** Myosin head with bound ADP + Pi binds to exposed actin active site. *(1 mark)*
3. **Power stroke:** Pi is released → conformational change → head pivots ~ 45° → actin slides past myosin → ADP released. *(1 mark)*
4. **Detachment:** A new ATP molecule binds to the myosin head → affinity for actin decreases → cross-bridge breaks. *(1 mark)*
5. **Re-cocking:** Myosin ATPase hydrolyzes ATP → ADP + Pi remain bound → head returns to high-energy perpendicular position, ready for the next cycle. *(1 mark)*
6. **ATP's Triple Role:** (i) Detaches myosin from actin; (ii) Hydrolyzed to re-cock the head; (iii) Powers the SERCA pump for Ca²⁺ reuptake (relaxation). *(1 mark)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b)

**Given:**
- Pushing force $F = 80$ N
- Steady speed $v = 0.6$ m/s
- Mass $m = 35$ kg (not directly needed for work since speed is steady → no acceleration)

**Step 1 — Mechanical Power**

$$P_{\text{mech}} = F \cdot v = 80 \cdot 0.6 = 48 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical Work per Minute**

$$W_{60\text{s}} = P_{\text{mech}} \cdot t = 48 \cdot 60 = 2880 \text{ J} = 2.88 \text{ kJ per minute}$$

**Step 3 — Metabolic Power**

$$P_{\text{met}} = \frac{P_{\text{mech}}}{\eta} = \frac{48}{0.20} = 240 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Intensity**

$240$ W metabolic power is **above the sustainable aerobic capacity** for an average worker (~ 75–100 W for sustained 8-hour work). However, since the task is **dynamic (steady speed, no static hold)**, fatigue is far less than Rohmert's static case. The task can be classified as **moderately heavy dynamic work** — acceptable in short cycles (< 30 min) with rest breaks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to **convert time units** when computing work per minute from power in watts. $1 \text{ W} = 1 \text{ J/s}$, so multiplying by 60 converts to J/min. Missing this loses 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3, Alternate Choice]*

**(a)** Explain **Rohmert's curve** for static muscular work. A factory task requires a worker to hold a 50 N tool with the shoulder. If the worker's maximum voluntary contraction (MVC) at the shoulder is 200 N, determine (i) the **% MVC**, (ii) the **predicted endurance time** using $K = 9$ min, and (iii) whether the task is safe for a 4-hour shift with 5-minute breaks every 30 minutes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **NIOSH Lifting Equation** with all six multipliers. A worker lifts a 20 kg load from a low pallet (V = 25 cm, H = 40 cm, A = 0°, F = 1 lift/6 min, D = 60 cm, good coupling). Compute the **RWL** and **Lifting Index (LI)**. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a)

**Rohmert's curve explanation (3 marks):**
Rohmert (1960) empirically established that endurance time during *static* (isometric) contractions decreases *hyperbolically* with the percentage of MVC. The empirical equation is:

$$t_{\text{end}} = K \cdot \left(\frac{100\%}{\%\text{MVC}} - 1\right)$$

At low forces (≤ 15 % MVC), endurance is essentially unlimited; beyond 50 % MVC, endurance drops below 2 minutes. Static contractions restrict blood flow → metabolic clearance is impaired → fatigue onset is rapid.

**Numerical Solution:**

**(i) % MVC:**

$$\%\text{MVC} = \frac{50}{200} \cdot 100\% = 25\%$$

**(ii) Endurance time:**

$$t_{\text{end}} = 9 \cdot \left(\frac{100}{25} - 1\right) = 9 \cdot (4 - 1) = 9 \cdot 3 = 27 \text{ minutes}$$

**(iii) Safety analysis for a 4-hour shift:**
- Each static hold of 27 minutes must be followed by rest.
- The proposed schedule (5 min rest every 30 min) **does NOT match** endurance — the worker will fatigue well before the 30-min mark.
- **Task is unsafe as designed.** Recommendations: (a) use a tool balancer to reduce effective load to ≤ 30 N (≈ 15 % MVC), (b) rotate workers, (c) introduce arm supports.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often misread the Rohmert equation as $t = K \cdot (\%\text{MVC})^{-1}$ instead of the correct form. Always include the "$-1$" — it represents the threshold below which no fatigue occurs.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b)

**NIOSH Lifting Equation Explanation (3 marks):**
The NIOSH Lifting Equation (Waters et al., 1993) computes a **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** based on six task multipliers:

$$\text{RWL} = \text{LC} \cdot \text{HM} \cdot \text{VM} \cdot \text{DM} \cdot \text{AM} \cdot \text{FM} \cdot \text{CM}$$

| Multiplier | Meaning | Range |
|------------|---------|-------|
| LC | Load Constant | 23 kg |
| HM | Horizontal Multiplier | $25/H$ |
| VM | Vertical Multiplier | $1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$ |
| DM | Distance Multiplier | $0.82 + 4.5/D$ |
| AM | Asymmetry Multiplier | $1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$ |
| FM | Frequency Multiplier | 0–1 |
| CM | Coupling Multiplier | 0.90–1.00 |

**Numerical Solution:**

- LC = 23 kg
- HM = $25 / 40 = 0.625$
- VM = $1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 25 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.15 = 0.850$
- DM = $0.82 + 4.5 / 60 = 0.82 + 0.075 = 0.895$
- AM = $1 - 0.0032 \cdot 0 = 1.000$
- FM = $1.00$ (1 lift/6 min, V = 25 cm, ≤ 8 hr)
- CM = $1.00$ (good coupling)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RWL} &= 23 \cdot 0.625 \cdot 0.850 \cdot 0.895 \cdot 1.000 \cdot 1.000 \cdot 1.000 \\
&= 23 \cdot 0.625 \cdot 0.850 \cdot 0.895
\end{aligned}$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$\begin{aligned}
23 \cdot 0.625 &= 14.375 \\
14.375 \cdot 0.850 &= 12.219 \\
12.219 \cdot 0.895 &= 10.936
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{RWL} \approx 10.94 \text{ kg}}$$

**Lifting Index:**

$$\text{LI} = \frac{20}{10.94} \approx 1.83$$

$$\boxed{\text{LI} \approx 1.83}$$

**Comment:** Since **LI ≈ 1.83 ≫ 1**, the task is **highly hazardous**. Over 75 % of the workforce faces elevated risk of lifting-related musculoskeletal disorders. Redesign recommendations: raise the pallet height (increase VM), reduce horizontal reach (increase HM), or use mechanical lifting aids.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently use $H = 25$ cm (the vertical value) instead of the **horizontal distance** in the HM formula. Always re-read — HM uses $H$ in cm, where $H$ is measured horizontally from the ankles to the hands.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Muscle hierarchy:** Muscle → Fascicle → Fiber → Myofibril → **Sarcomere**.
- [x] **Sliding Filament Theory:** Filaments **do not shorten**; they slide; A-band constant; I-band & H-zone shrink.
- [x] **Sarcomere zones:** Z-line, A-band, I-band, H-zone, M-line — know boundaries.
- [x] **Three muscle types:** Skeletal (voluntary), Smooth (involuntary), Cardiac (involuntary) — relevance to HFE is mostly **skeletal**.
- [x] **Contractile proteins:** Actin (thin, has troponin/tropomyosin), Myosin (thick, has ATPase heads).
- [x] **EC Coupling chain:** AP → ACh → DHP → Ryanodine → Ca²⁺ release → Troponin-C → Tropomyosin shift → Cross-bridge.
- [x] **Cross-bridge cycle:** Attach → Power stroke → Detach (ATP) → Re-cock (ATP hydrolysis). All four steps require ATP *in some form*.
- [x] **Fiber types:**
   - **Type I** = slow, red, fatigue-resistant, posture, low force.
   - **Type IIa** = intermediate.
   - **Type IIx** = fast, white, fatigable, high force.
- [x] **Henneman's Size Principle:** Recruitment order = Type I → IIa → IIx (small to large).
- [x] **Hill's Force–Velocity Equation:** $(P + a)(v + b) = \text{const}$; peak power at ~ 0.3 $v_{\max}$.
- [x] **Length–Tension:** Max tension at optimal sarcomere length (≈ 2.0–2.2 µm).
- [x] **Rohmert's Endurance:** $t_{\text{end}} = K \cdot \left(\dfrac{100\%}{\%\text{MVC}} - 1\right)$; **15 % MVC = safe static guideline**.
- [x] **NIOSH Lifting Equation:** 6 multipliers × LC (23 kg); LI > 1 ⇒ redesign.
- [x] **Mechanical efficiency:** Skeletal muscle peak ≈ 20–25 %.
- [x] **Rigor mortis:** Caused by lack of ATP — cross-bridges cannot detach.
- [x] **Three ATP roles in muscle:** (1) Cross-bridge detachment, (2) Myosin head re-cocking, (3) SERCA pump for Ca²⁺ reuptake.
- [x] **Ergonomic redesign triggers:** LI > 1; static task > 15 % MVC; repetitive cycle < recommended frequency per ISO 11226.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"A-band constant, I-band shrinks, ATP detaches, Ca²⁺ triggers, and Rohmert never lies."* Master these five micro-facts and 80 % of Module 3 numerical/short questions become free marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Physiology; Measure of Physiological Strain, Physical Workload, Strength & Endurance; Manual Material Handling (MMH), Bio-mechanical Recommended Limits of MMH; Reducing the Risk of MMH Overexertion; General Principles of Biomechanical Design; Anthropometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Work Physiology; Measure of Physiological Strain, Physical Workload, Strength & Endurance; Manual Material Handling (MMH), Bio-mechanical Recommended Limits of MMH; Reducing the Risk of MMH Overexertion; General Principles of Biomechanical Design; Anthropometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Work Physiology

**Work Physiology** is the branch of ergonomics and human factors engineering that studies how the human body responds physiologically (cardiovascular, respiratory, muscular, and metabolic) to physical work demands imposed by the workplace. It quantifies the strain experienced by the worker when performing tasks such as lifting, pushing, walking, climbing, or repetitive manual operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):** *Work physiology is the scientific study of the energy transformations, cardiovascular responses, and muscular activities of the human body during the performance of occupational work, used to evaluate physical workload and prevent fatigue, overexertion, and occupational disorders.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Engine" Model
Think of the human body as a **hybrid engine** that runs on oxygen (aerobic) and stored glycogen (anaerobic). Just like a car engine has a **redline RPM** (maximum safe speed), the human body has a **maximum oxygen uptake (VO₂ max)** and a **maximum heart rate (HR max)**. When the "engine" of a worker is revved beyond its sustainable redline for too long, the "engine" begins to overheat, leak oil, and eventually break down — which, in humans, manifests as **fatigue, musculoskeletal disorders, and cardiovascular strain**.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard unit of energy in work physiology is the **kilocalorie (kcal)** or **watt (W)**. The standard measure of aerobic capacity is **VO₂ max** expressed in **mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹**.

---

## 1.2 Physiological Strain and Its Measures

**Physiological strain** is the response of the body to a physical workload. It can be quantified through several measurable indicators:

| Measure | Symbol | Unit | What it Tells Us |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heart Rate | HR | beats per minute (bpm) | Cardiovascular load |
| Oxygen Consumption | VO₂ | mL·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹ | Aerobic energy demand |
| Energy Expenditure | E | kcal/min or W | Total metabolic cost |
| Respiratory Quotient | RQ | ratio (VCO₂/VO₂) | Fuel source (carb vs fat) |
| Body Core Temperature | T_core | °C | Heat strain |
| Blood Lactate | [La⁻] | mmol/L | Anaerobic threshold |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cardiovascular Load (% HR reserve) is the single most widely used field measure of physiological strain in industrial ergonomics.**

---

## 1.3 Physical Workload, Strength, and Endurance

- **Physical Workload**: The external demand placed on the worker by the task (force, frequency, duration, posture).
- **Muscular Strength**: The maximum force a muscle group can exert in a single voluntary contraction (one-rep max concept).
- **Muscular Endurance**: The ability of a muscle or muscle group to sustain repeated contractions or hold a contraction over time.
- **Cardiorespiratory Endurance (Aerobic Capacity)**: The body's ability to take in, transport, and use oxygen during sustained physical activity.

### Analogy — Strength vs. Endurance
**Strength** is like a **single powerful pull of a bowstring** (a sprinter's burst). **Endurance** is like **walking steadily for 8 hours** (a postal worker's day). Both are needed, but a task like lifting a 25 kg box repeatedly requires *both* in balance.

---

## 1.4 Manual Material Handling (MMH)

**Manual Material Handling (MMH)** refers to any activity where a worker physically moves, supports, restrains, or carries a load using their own body force — including **lifting, lowering, pushing, pulling, carrying, and holding** of objects. MMH is the leading cause of occupational back injuries worldwide.

> [!NOTE]
> Approximately **one-third** of all workplace injury compensation claims globally are linked to MMH activities, with low-back disorders (LBDs) being the most prevalent outcome.

---

## 1.5 Biomechanical Recommended Limits of MMH

The **NIOSH (National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health, USA, 1981; revised 1994)** established the scientific framework for recommended weight limits during manual lifting tasks. The key outputs are:

- **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** — the load that nearly all healthy workers can lift safely.
- **Lifting Index (LI)** — the ratio of actual load to RWL; **LI > 1.0** indicates elevated risk.

The standard **Load Constant (LC)** is **23 kg (51 lb)**.

---

## 1.6 Anthropometry

**Anthropometry** is the scientific study of the **measurements of the human body** — dimensions, masses, volumes, and inertial properties. It provides the quantitative foundation for designing workstations, tools, controls, protective equipment, and workspaces that fit the user population.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anthropometric Principle:** A workplace must accommodate a range of users — typically the **5th percentile (small) to 95th percentile (large)** — to ensure inclusivity and safety.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Door Frame Rule"
Imagine designing a doorway. If you only design it for the **50th percentile** (average), short people bang their heads and tall people duck — wait, tall people don't fit! The same logic applies to designing a driver's cabin, an assembly table, or a pair of safety gloves.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physiological Strain — The Cardiovascular Chain of Response

When a worker begins a physical task, the following sequence unfolds:

1. **Central Command** (Cerebral Cortex) issues motor signals.
2. **Heart Rate (HR)** rises to deliver more oxygenated blood.
3. **Stroke Volume (SV)** increases to pump more blood per beat.
4. **Cardiac Output (CO = HR × SV)** increases — measured in **L/min**.
5. **Pulmonary Ventilation (VE)** rises to intake more oxygen.
6. **Oxygen Consumption (VO₂)** increases to meet muscle demand.
7. **Body Core Temperature** rises, triggering sweating and heat dissipation.
8. **Blood Lactate** accumulates if intensity exceeds aerobic threshold.

> The body maintains **homeostasis** (steady internal state) by balancing these responses. When the rate of demand exceeds the rate of supply, **strain** manifests as fatigue.

---

## 2.2 Key Formulas — Physiological Strain

### A. Working Heart Rate (% HR reserve, Karvonen Formula)

$$
\%HR_{reserve} = \frac{HR_{work} - HR_{rest}}{HR_{max} - HR_{rest}} \times 100
$$

Where:
- $HR_{work}$ = heart rate during work
- $HR_{rest}$ = resting heart rate
- $HR_{max} \approx 220 - \text{age}$ (in years)

### B. Oxygen Consumption Relative to Maximum

$$
\%VO_2_{max} = \frac{VO_2_{work}}{VO_2_{max}} \times 100
$$

### C. Net (Working) Oxygen Consumption

$$
VO_2_{net} = VO_2_{work} - VO_2_{rest}
$$

### D. Energy Expenditure from Oxygen

$$
E \text{ (kcal/min)} = VO_2 \text{ (L/min)} \times 5.0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **5.0 kcal per litre of O₂** assumes a mixed-fuel (carbohydrate + fat) metabolism with an RQ of ~0.85.

### E. Acceptable Workload Limits (Industrial Standards)

| Strain Indicator | Light Work | Moderate Work | Heavy Work | Very Heavy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heart rate (bpm) | < 90 | 90 – 110 | 110 – 130 | > 130 |
| Oxygen uptake (L/min) | < 0.5 | 0.5 – 1.0 | 1.0 – 1.5 | > 1.5 |
| Energy (kcal/min) | < 2.5 | 2.5 – 5.0 | 5.0 – 7.5 | > 7.5 |
| % VO₂ max | < 25% | 25 – 50% | 50 – 75% | > 75% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an 8-hour shift, oxygen consumption should **not exceed 35% of VO₂ max** for sustained work to avoid cumulative fatigue.

---

## 2.3 Strength and Endurance

### Static (Isometric) Strength

Static strength is measured as the **maximum force** a worker can hold for a few seconds in a given posture. It declines sharply with:

- **Deviation from neutral posture** (the further from neutral, the weaker the joint).
- **Sustained duration** (a 1-minute hold is far less force than a 1-second hold).

### Dynamic (Isotonic) Strength
The **one-repetition maximum (1-RM)** is the gold standard for dynamic strength measurement.

### Endurance (Holding Time, Rohmert's Curve)

$$
\text{Holding Time (s)} = k \times \left(\frac{\text{Maximum Strength}}{\text{Required Force}}\right)^{p}
$$

Typical constants: $k \approx 12$ s, $p \approx 2$ to 3.

> **Work Physiology Rule:** As the **required force increases** above 15–20% of maximum, **endurance time drops exponentially**.

---

## 2.4 NIOSH Lifting Equation — Complete Formula Set

The **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** for a manual lifting task is:

$$
RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM
$$

**Load Constant (LC):** $LC = 23 \text{ kg}$ (51 lb)

### Multiplier Formulas

| Multiplier | Symbol | Formula | Range / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Horizontal Multiplier | $HM$ | $HM = \dfrac{25}{H}$ | $H$ = horizontal distance from spine to hands (cm); $25 \le H \le 63$ |
| Vertical Multiplier | $VM$ | $VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$ | $V$ = vertical height of hands (cm); $0 \le V \le 175$ |
| Distance Multiplier | $DM$ | $DM = 0.82 + \dfrac{4.5}{D}$ | $D$ = vertical travel distance (cm); $25 \le D \le 175$ |
| Asymmetry Multiplier | $AM$ | $AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$ | $A$ = asymmetry angle in degrees; $0 \le A \le 135$ |
| Frequency Multiplier | $FM$ | From NIOSH Frequency Table | Depends on lifts/min × duration |
| Coupling Multiplier | $CM$ | 1.0 / 0.95 / 0.90 | Good / Fair / Poor hand-to-object coupling |

### Lifting Index (LI)

$$
LI = \frac{L}{RWL}
$$

Where $L$ = actual load lifted.

### Risk Classification

| Lifting Index (LI) | Risk Level | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|
| LI ≤ 1.0 | Low | Acceptable for most workers |
| 1.0 < LI ≤ 3.0 | Moderate | Engineering / administrative controls needed |
| LI > 3.0 | High | Task redesign is mandatory |

---

## 2.5 Strength–Endurance Engineering Implications

> [!NOTE]
> A worker can sustain roughly **8–15% of maximum static strength** for an entire 8-hour shift without local muscular fatigue. This is the basis of the **Niosh / Rohmert Endurance Curve**.

The **Maximum Voluntary Contraction (MVC)** is the design boundary for any sustained static load.

---

## 2.6 General Principles of Biomechanical Design

These principles prevent musculoskeletal disorders by reducing **moment arms, joint torques, and contact stress**:

1. **Minimize Moment Arms** — Keep loads close to the body's center of gravity (L5/S1 disc).
2. **Use Strong, Large Muscle Groups** — Legs > back > arms (in that descending order of safe force).
3. **Avoid Static Postures** — Provide for dynamic movement and posture change.
4. **Keep Joints in Neutral** — Avoid extreme flexion, extension, abduction, or rotation.
5. **Reduce Forceful Exertions** — Mechanical aids (hoists, dollies) wherever possible.
6. **Avoid High-Frequency Repetition** — Cycle times > 30 s preferred; < 30 s increases risk.
7. **Provide Adequate Recovery Time** — Allow micro-pauses between exertions.
8. **Match Demands to Capability** — Use strength data, age, and gender statistics.

---

## 2.7 Anthropometry — Design Categories

| Category | Examples | Design Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Static (Structural) Dimensions** | Stature, eye height, shoulder height | Doorways, cabin heights |
| **Dynamic (Functional) Dimensions** | Reach, leg length, grip span | Workstation layouts |
| **Strength-Related** | Grip strength, push/pull force | Tool and control design |
| **Body Surface Area** | Skin surface (m²) | Ventilation, clothing design |
| **Body Segments** | Trunk length, thigh length, foot breadth | Seating, console design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **5th–95th percentile range** is the ergonomic design standard (accommodates 90% of the working population).

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Printable Summary Table)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | % HR reserve | $\frac{HR_{w} - HR_{r}}{HR_{max} - HR_{r}} \times 100$ | % | Karvonen equation |
| 2 | HR max estimate | $220 - \text{age}$ | bpm | Population average |
| 3 | % VO₂ max | $\frac{VO_2^{work}}{VO_2^{max}} \times 100$ | % | Aerobic strain |
| 4 | Energy from O₂ | $E = VO_2 \times 5.0$ | kcal/min | Mixed-fuel |
| 5 | NIOSH RWL | $LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$ | kg | $LC = 23$ kg |
| 6 | Horizontal Multiplier | $HM = 25 / H$ | – | $H$ in cm |
| 7 | Vertical Multiplier | $VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$ | – | $V$ in cm |
| 8 | Distance Multiplier | $DM = 0.82 + 4.5 / D$ | – | $D$ in cm |
| 9 | Asymmetry Multiplier | $AM = 1 - 0.0032A$ | – | $A$ in degrees |
| 10 | Lifting Index | $LI = L / RWL$ | – | $LI > 1$ = risk |
| 11 | Holding Time | $t = k \cdot (MVC / F)^p$ | seconds | Rohmert's model |
| 12 | Cardiac Output | $CO = HR \times SV$ | L/min | Heart performance |

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Engineering**: Designing assembly lines with optimal cycle times that keep workers below 35% VO₂ max.
- **Vehicle Design**: Setting seat heights, pedal distances, and dashboard reach using 5th–95th percentile anthropometry.
- **PPE Design**: Sizing gloves, helmets, and harnesses to body dimensions.
- **Healthcare**: Preventing nurse back injuries by applying the RWL / LI framework to patient lifting.
- **Construction**: Scaffolding and concrete formwork design to reduce stooped lifting.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) Components

The NIOSH equation was developed from a **biomechanical, physiological, and psychophysical** synthesis of safe lifting criteria. Each multiplier corresponds to one of these three criterion families:

- **HM & VM** → Biomechanical criterion (compressive force on L5/S1 disc ≤ 3400 N for 99% of workers)
- **DM & AM** → Biomechanical criterion (trunk moment, shear force)
- **FM** → Physiological criterion (energy expenditure ≤ 3.5 kcal/min sustained)
- **CM** → Psychophysical criterion (worker-perceived acceptable weight)

**LC = 23 kg** is the baseline weight that satisfies all three criteria simultaneously under **reference conditions**:

$$
H = 25 \text{ cm}, \quad V = 75 \text{ cm}, \quad D = 25 \text{ cm}, \quad A = 0°, \quad F = 1 \text{ lift/min}, \quad C = \text{good}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Complete NIOSH RWL Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A worker in a warehouse lifts a **15 kg box** from a shelf at **80 cm height** to a conveyor at **50 cm height**. The horizontal distance from the spine is **40 cm**. The lift is **asymmetric by 45°** to the right. The worker lifts **3 times per minute for a 2-hour period**. Coupling is **fair**. Calculate the **RWL, LI, and risk classification**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify inputs**

- Load $L = 15$ kg
- Horizontal distance $H = 40$ cm
- Vertical origin $V_L = 50$ cm (lower position — wait, lifting means going from low to high)

Let $V_{origin} = 50$ cm (start), $V_{destination} = 80$ cm (end). NIOSH takes $V$ as the **vertical location of the hands at the start of the lift** (origin).

- $V = 50$ cm
- Travel distance $D = V_{destination} - V_{origin} = 80 - 50 = 30$ cm
- Asymmetry $A = 45°$
- Frequency $F = 3$ lifts/min, Duration = 2 hr → Look up FM

For $\le$ 1 hr duration and 3 lifts/min, **FM = 0.45** (from NIOSH table for ≤ 1 hour).
Since duration is 2 hr, use the **2-hour column**: at 3 lifts/min, **FM = 0.40**.

- Coupling $C$ = fair → **CM = 0.95**

**Step 2: Compute each multiplier**

$$
HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625
$$

$$
VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 50 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \times 25 = 1 - 0.075 = 0.925
$$

$$
DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D} = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{30} = 0.82 + 0.15 = 0.97
$$

$$
AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A = 1 - 0.0032 \times 45 = 1 - 0.144 = 0.856
$$

$FM = 0.40$ (from NIOSH table)

$CM = 0.95$

**Step 3: Compute RWL**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL &= LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM \\
&= 23 \times 0.625 \times 0.925 \times 0.97 \times 0.856 \times 0.40 \times 0.95
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL_{partial_1} &= 23 \times 0.625 = 14.375 \text{ kg} \\
RWL_{partial_2} &= 14.375 \times 0.925 = 13.297 \text{ kg} \\
RWL_{partial_3} &= 13.297 \times 0.97 = 12.898 \text{ kg} \\
RWL_{partial_4} &= 12.898 \times 0.856 = 11.041 \text{ kg} \\
RWL_{partial_5} &= 11.041 \times 0.40 = 4.416 \text{ kg} \\
RWL_{partial_6} &= 4.416 \times 0.95 = 4.195 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{RWL \approx 4.20 \text{ kg}}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Lifting Index**

$$
LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{15}{4.20} = 3.57
$$

**Step 5: Risk Classification**

Since $LI = 3.57 > 3.0$, the task is **HIGH RISK** and **task redesign is mandatory**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The actual load (15 kg) is **3.57 times** the scientifically safe limit (4.20 kg). The high asymmetry, fair coupling, and frequency all contribute to the severely reduced RWL.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Karvonen Cardiovascular Strain Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A 35-year-old male assembly worker has a resting heart rate of **72 bpm**. While performing a riveting task, his working heart rate is measured at **120 bpm**. Calculate the **% HR reserve (cardiovascular strain)** and classify the workload.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Estimate HR max**

$$
HR_{max} = 220 - \text{age} = 220 - 35 = 185 \text{ bpm}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Karvonen formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%HR_{reserve} &= \frac{HR_{work} - HR_{rest}}{HR_{max} - HR_{rest}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{120 - 72}{185 - 72} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{48}{113} \times 100 \\
&= 42.48\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Classify workload**

$$
\boxed{\%HR_{reserve} \approx 42.5\% \Rightarrow \text{Moderate Work}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For an 8-hour shift, sustained cardiovascular load should not exceed **30–35% of HR reserve** for healthy industrial workers.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Energy Expenditure from Oxygen Consumption

**Problem Statement:**
A construction worker has a resting VO₂ of 0.30 L/min and a working VO₂ of 1.45 L/min. Calculate the **net oxygen consumption** and the **energy expenditure**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Net oxygen consumption**

$$
VO_2^{net} = VO_2^{work} - VO_2^{rest} = 1.45 - 0.30 = 1.15 \text{ L/min}
$$

**Step 2: Energy expenditure**

$$
E = VO_2 \times 5.0 = 1.15 \times 5.0 = 5.75 \text{ kcal/min}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to watts**

$$
E = 5.75 \times 69.78 = 401.2 \text{ W} \approx 0.40 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The thermochemical conversion **1 kcal/min = 69.78 W** is used to convert from metabolic units to mechanical power units.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Rohmert Holding Endurance Time

**Problem Statement:**
A worker must hold a **30 N tool** in a horizontal position. The maximum strength (MVC) in that posture is **150 N**. Using Rohmert's constants $k = 12$ s, $p = 2.5$, calculate the **maximum holding time**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply Rohmert's equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= k \cdot \left(\frac{MVC}{F}\right)^p \\
&= 12 \times \left(\frac{150}{30}\right)^{2.5} \\
&= 12 \times (5)^{2.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $5^{2.5}$**

$$
5^{2.5} = 5^2 \times 5^{0.5} = 25 \times \sqrt{5} = 25 \times 2.236 = 55.90
$$

**Step 3: Final calculation**

$$
t = 12 \times 55.90 = 670.8 \text{ s} \approx 11.18 \text{ min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Holding endurance} \approx 11.2 \text{ minutes}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse $F/MVC$ vs $MVC/F$. The correct ratio is $MVC/F$ in the numerator. Using the inverse will **grossly underestimate** the holding time.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Anthropometric Workspace Design

**Problem Statement:**
A control panel for an industrial machine must be reachable by **at least 90% of the working population**. The 5th percentile **forward arm reach** (sitting, shoulder pivoting) is **65 cm**, and the 95th percentile is **90 cm**. Where should the most important control be placed?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply anthropometric principle**

For a control used by **all** workers, place it within the reach of the **5th percentile** (smallest reach) when sitting:

$$
\text{Optimal forward reach} \le 65 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2: For critical / frequent controls**

Place the **most-used control at the 5th percentile forward reach** to ensure everyone can reach it. The **least-used controls** may be placed at the 95th percentile reach (up to 90 cm) for occasional use.

**Step 3: Optimal horizontal height**

The **5th percentile elbow rest height** = 18 cm, **95th percentile** = 33 cm. Average ≈ 25 cm. A keyboard/control panel should be just above this — typically at **5th percentile shoulder–elbow height**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Population accommodation rule:** Design the **lower bound** by the 5th percentile and the **upper bound** by the 95th percentile for inclusive design.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Strain Indicators and Their Validity

| Strain Indicator | Type | Validity | Invasive? | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heart Rate | Cardiovascular | High | No | Field studies |
| % HR Reserve | Cardiovascular | High | No | Job screening |
| VO₂ | Metabolic | Very High | No (with mask) | Lab work |
| Energy Expenditure | Metabolic | High | No | Task design |
| EMG | Muscle activity | High | Surface: No | Local fatigue |
| Body Core Temp | Thermal | High | Yes (pill) | Heat stress |
| Lactate | Anaerobic | Moderate | Yes (blood) | Threshold analysis |
| RPE (Borg Scale) | Subjective | Moderate | No | Quick screening |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Work Physiology — The Physiological Strain Cascade

This block diagram shows the cause–effect flow of physiological strain during physical work.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Demand / Physical Workload] --> B[Central Nervous System Activation]
    B --> C[Heart Rate Rises]
    C --> D[Stroke Volume Increases]
    D --> E[Cardiac Output Increases]
    B --> F[Ventilation Increases]
    F --> G[Oxygen Uptake VO2 Rises]
    E --> H[Muscle Oxygen Delivery]
    G --> H
    H --> I[ATP Resynthesis in Muscle]
    I --> J[Mechanical Work Output]
    I --> K[Heat Production]
    K --> L[Core Temperature Rise]
    L --> M[Sweating and Heat Loss]
    I --> N[Lactate Accumulation if VO2 Insufficient]
    N --> O[Muscular Fatigue]
    H --> P[Cardiovascular Drift Over Time]
    P --> Q[Sustained Physiological Strain]
    O --> Q
    Q --> R[Reduced Performance and Risk of Injury]
    R --> S[Task Redesign Required]
```

## 4.2 NIOSH Lifting Equation — Multiplier Decision Flow

A block-level flow for the NIOSH Lifting Equation computation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Lifting Task] --> B[Measure H Horizontal Distance cm]
    A --> C[Measure V Vertical Location cm]
    A --> D[Measure D Travel Distance cm]
    A --> E[Measure A Asymmetry Angle degrees]
    A --> F[Identify F Frequency lifts per min]
    A --> G[Identify Duration hours]
    A --> H[Identify Coupling Quality]
    B --> I[Compute HM 25 divided by H]
    C --> J[Compute VM 1 minus 0.003 times mod V minus 75]
    D --> K[Compute DM 0.82 plus 4.5 divided by D]
    E --> L[Compute AM 1 minus 0.0032 times A]
    F --> G
    G --> M[Look up FM from NIOSH Table]
    H --> N[Assign CM 1.0 or 0.95 or 0.90]
    I --> O[Multiply All Factors with LC 23 kg]
    J --> O
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Obtain RWL in kg]
    A --> Q[Identify Actual Load L in kg]
    Q --> R[Compute LI equals L divided by RWL]
    P --> R
    R --> S{LI greater than 3.0}
    S -->|Yes| T[HIGH RISK Redesign Mandatory]
    S -->|No| U{LI greater than 1.0}
    U -->|Yes| V[MODERATE RISK Controls Needed]
    U -->|No| W[LOW RISK Task Acceptable]
```

## 4.3 General Principles of Biomechanical Design — Priority Hierarchy

A hierarchical block diagram of design intervention priorities.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomechanical Design Goal: Reduce Musculoskeletal Injury Risk] --> B[Level 1: Eliminate the Hazard]
    B --> B1[Use Mechanical Aids Hoists Conveyors]
    B1 --> C[Level 2: Engineering Controls]
    C --> C1[Redesign Workstation to Minimize Reach]
    C1 --> C2[Adjust Work Surface Height]
    C2 --> C3[Reduce Load Weight]
    C3 --> D[Level 3: Administrative Controls]
    D --> D1[Job Rotation]
    D1 --> D2[Rest Pauses]
    D2 --> D3[Reduce Lifting Frequency]
    D3 --> E[Level 4: PPE and Training]
    E --> E1[Back Belts Limited Evidence]
    E1 --> E2[Worker Training in Lifting Technique]
    E2 --> F[Continuous Improvement Loop]
    F --> A
```

## 4.4 Anthropometry — Design Application Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Anthropometric Database] --> B[Static Dimensions]
    A --> C[Dynamic Dimensions]
    A --> D[Strength Data]
    A --> E[Body Segments]
    B --> B1[Stature Eye Height Shoulder Height]
    B1 --> F1[Cabin Doorway Overhead Clearance Design]
    C --> C1[Forward Reach Grip Span Leg Length]
    C1 --> F2[Workstation Console Layout Design]
    D --> D1[Grip Strength Push Pull Torque]
    D1 --> F3[Tool and Control Design]
    E --> E1[Trunk Thigh Foot Breadth]
    E1 --> F4[Seating and Console Dimensions]
    F1 --> G[Inclusive Workspace Accommodating 5th to 95th Percentile]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MMH Overexertion Risk Identified] --> B[Quantify Risk using NIOSH RWL and LI]
    B --> C{LI less than 1.0}

    C -->|No| E{Engineering Controls Feasible}
    E -->|Yes| F[Implement Mechanical Lifts Dollies Conveyors]
    E -->|No| G{Administrative Controls Feasible}
    G -->|Yes| H[Job Rotation Team Lifting Reduce Frequency]
    G -->|No| I[Worker Selection Based on Strength Data]

    F --> K[Re-evaluate RWL and LI]

    J --> K
    K --> L{LI less than 1.0}

    L -->|No| N[Escalate to Higher-Level Intervention]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Work Physiology. List any four measures used to quantify physiological strain in industrial workers.**

**Model Answer:**

**Work Physiology** is the branch of human factors engineering that studies the **physiological responses** of the human body (cardiovascular, respiratory, muscular, metabolic) to physical work demands, to evaluate workload and prevent fatigue and injury.

**Four measures of physiological strain:**

1. **Heart Rate (HR)** — measured in bpm; indicates cardiovascular load.
2. **Oxygen Consumption (VO₂)** — measured in L/min; indicates aerobic energy demand.
3. **Energy Expenditure (E)** — measured in kcal/min; indicates total metabolic cost.
4. **Body Core Temperature** — measured in °C; indicates heat strain.

> *(Any 4 of: HR, VO₂, Energy Expenditure, Respiratory Quotient, Blood Lactate, RPE, EMG)*

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the NIOSH Load Constant? Why is its value fixed at 23 kg?**

**Model Answer:**

The **NIOSH Load Constant (LC) = 23 kg (51 lb)** is the baseline weight used in the NIOSH Lifting Equation, representing the maximum recommended weight that nearly all healthy workers can lift under **reference (ideal) conditions**:
- Horizontal distance $H = 25$ cm
- Vertical location $V = 75$ cm
- Travel distance $D = 25$ cm
- Asymmetry $A = 0°$
- Frequency $F \le 1$ lift/min
- Duration $\le 1$ hour
- Coupling = Good

The value **23 kg** was determined by simultaneously satisfying three independent criteria:
- **Biomechanical** — L5/S1 disc compression force $\le$ 3400 N
- **Physiological** — Energy expenditure $\le$ 3.5 kcal/min
- **Psychophysical** — Worker-acceptable load (e.g., 75th percentile of perceived acceptable weight)

Under ideal conditions, all three criteria converge to ≈ 23 kg. Under real conditions, the **multipliers (HM, VM, DM, AM, FM, CM)** reduce this value to the actual RWL.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Module 3, Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define **Manual Material Handling (MMH)**. Explain the **NIOSH Lifting Equation** in detail, listing all six multipliers with their formulas and safe operating ranges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker lifts a **12 kg load** from a knuckle height of **90 cm** to shoulder height of **140 cm**. The horizontal distance from the spine to the hands is **30 cm**. The lift is **asymmetric by 30°**. The frequency is **2 lifts/min for 1 hour**. Coupling is **good**.

Calculate the **RWL, LI**, and classify the risk. Suggest **two engineering interventions** to reduce the risk if LI > 1.0. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

**Definition of MMH:** Manual Material Handling refers to any workplace activity in which a worker **lifts, lowers, pushes, pulls, carries, holds, or restrains** a load using bodily force. It is the single largest cause of occupational back injuries globally.

**NIOSH Lifting Equation:**

$$
RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM
$$

**LC** = 23 kg (Load Constant)

| Multiplier | Formula | Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| $HM$ | $25 / H$ | $H$ = horizontal distance, $25 \le H \le 63$ cm |
| $VM$ | $1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$ | $V$ = vertical height, $0 \le V \le 175$ cm |
| $DM$ | $0.82 + 4.5 / D$ | $D$ = travel distance, $25 \le D \le 175$ cm |
| $AM$ | $1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$ | $A$ = asymmetry angle, $0 \le A \le 135°$ |
| $FM$ | Frequency table (NIOSH) | Depends on lifts/min and duration |
| $CM$ | 1.0 / 0.95 / 0.90 | Good / Fair / Poor coupling |

**Lifting Index:** $LI = L / RWL$; $LI \le 1$ is acceptable; $LI > 1$ indicates increasing risk.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Given:** $L = 12$ kg, $V_{origin} = 90$ cm, $V_{dest} = 140$ cm, $H = 30$ cm, $A = 30°$, $F = 2$ lifts/min, Duration = 1 hr, $C$ = good.

**Step 1: Compute travel distance**

$$
D = V_{dest} - V_{origin} = 140 - 90 = 50 \text{ cm}
$$

**Step 2: Compute each multiplier**

$$
HM = \frac{25}{30} = 0.833
$$

$$
VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 90 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \times 15 = 1 - 0.045 = 0.955
$$

$$
DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{50} = 0.82 + 0.09 = 0.91
$$

$$
AM = 1 - 0.0032 \times 30 = 1 - 0.096 = 0.904
$$

For $F = 2$ lifts/min and Duration $\le$ 1 hr, $FM = 0.50$ (from NIOSH table).

$CM = 1.0$ (good coupling).

**Step 3: Compute RWL**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL &= 23 \times 0.833 \times 0.955 \times 0.91 \times 0.904 \times 0.50 \times 1.0 \\
&= 23 \times 0.833 = 19.16 \\
&\times 0.955 = 18.30 \\
&\times 0.91 = 16.65 \\
&\times 0.904 = 15.05 \\
&\times 0.50 = 7.53 \\
&\times 1.0 = 7.53 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{RWL \approx 7.53 \text{ kg}}
$$

**Step 4: Compute LI**

$$
LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{12}{7.53} = 1.59
$$

**Step 5: Risk classification**

$$
LI = 1.59 \in (1.0, 3.0) \Rightarrow \textbf{Moderate Risk}
$$

**Step 6: Two engineering interventions**

1. **Reduce vertical travel** by repositioning the destination shelf closer to origin (reduces D, increases DM, raises RWL).
2. **Eliminate asymmetry** by repositioning the worker and shelves so that the lift is in the sagittal plane (reduces A to 0°, raises AM and RWL).

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam – Module 3, Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Define **Physical Workload, Strength, and Endurance**. Derive the **Karvonen formula for %HR reserve** and explain its significance in workplace workload assessment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 40-year-old worker has a **resting heart rate of 70 bpm**. During a material handling task, his **working heart rate is 135 bpm**. Calculate the **% HR reserve** and classify the workload. The same worker has a **VO₂ max of 3.5 L/min** and a **working VO₂ of 2.1 L/min**. Calculate the **% VO₂ max** and **energy expenditure**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Physical Workload:** The external demands (force, frequency, duration, posture) imposed on a worker by a task.

**Strength:** The maximum force a muscle or muscle group can exert in a single voluntary contraction (one-rep max).

**Endurance:** The ability of muscles to sustain repeated contractions or hold a load over time (depends on aerobic capacity and local muscle fatigue).

**Derivation of Karvonen Formula:**

The classical method of estimating heart rate reserve uses the **range between maximum and resting heart rate**. The proportion of this range used during work is:

$$
\%HR_{reserve} = \frac{HR_{work} - HR_{rest}}{HR_{max} - HR_{rest}} \times 100
$$

**Derivation rationale:** Heart rate does not begin at zero during rest; it has a baseline $HR_{rest}$. The available reserve for work is $HR_{max} - HR_{rest}$. The working heart rate consumes $(HR_{work} - HR_{rest})$ of this reserve. Dividing by the total reserve and multiplying by 100 gives the percentage utilization.

**Significance in workplace assessment:**

- Provides a **normalized, age-independent** measure of cardiovascular strain.
- Used to **classify workload** (light < 30%, moderate 30–50%, heavy 50–75%, very heavy > 75%).
- For an **8-hour shift**, sustained work should keep %HR reserve **below 30–35%** to avoid cumulative fatigue.
- Used in **worker screening** (e.g., those with low cardiovascular fitness may need reassignment).
- Avoids absolute heart rate comparisons (which vary with age, fitness, and individual differences).

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Step 1: Estimate HR max**

$$
HR_{max} = 220 - \text{age} = 220 - 40 = 180 \text{ bpm}
$$

**Step 2: % HR reserve**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%HR_{reserve} &= \frac{135 - 70}{180 - 70} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{65}{110} \times 100 \\
&= 59.09\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\%HR_{reserve} \approx 59.1\% \Rightarrow \text{Heavy Work}}
$$

**Step 3: % VO₂ max**

$$
\%VO_2^{max} = \frac{VO_2^{work}}{VO_2^{max}} \times 100 = \frac{2.1}{3.5} \times 100 = 60.0\%
$$

**Step 4: Energy expenditure**

$$
E = VO_2 \times 5.0 = 2.1 \times 5.0 = 10.5 \text{ kcal/min}
$$

**Step 5: Convert to watts**

$$
E = 10.5 \times 69.78 = 732.7 \text{ W} \approx 0.733 \text{ kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 10.5 \text{ kcal/min } \approx 733 \text{ W (Heavy Work)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical):**
> 1. **RWL formula mistakes** — Students often forget to include ALL six multipliers, especially FM and CM. A typical 14-mark question awards 1 mark per multiplier. Missing one = -1 mark, capped at 7 marks for the formula.
> 2. **Unit confusion in VO₂** — Some students report VO₂ in mL/min instead of L/min, which causes a 1000× error in energy. Always convert: **1 L = 1000 mL**.
> 3. **Karvonen formula inverted** — A common error is to use $(HR_{max} - HR_{work}) / (HR_{max} - HR_{rest})$, which yields the **% HR reserve REMAINING** — a meaningless quantity. The numerator MUST be $HR_{work} - HR_{rest}$.
> 4. **Lifting Index sign-off** — The classification bands $(LI \le 1, 1<LI\le 3, LI > 3)$ are often misremembered as 1.5 and 2.5. Memorize correctly: **1.0 and 3.0** are the threshold values.
> 5. **Asymmetry angle unit** — Asymmetry is measured in **degrees**, not radians. Using radians will give a wildly wrong AM (close to 1.0).
> 6. **NCM vs. LI confusion** — NCM is *not* a NIOSH multiplier; sometimes students invent a "Coupling Normalization Constant." Only six multipliers exist.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Work Physiology** quantifies the body's responses (HR, VO₂, E) to physical work; aim is to prevent fatigue and musculoskeletal injury.
- **Physiological strain** is best measured by **% HR reserve (Karvonen)**, **% VO₂ max**, and **energy expenditure**; sustained limits: $\le 30\% HR_{reserve}$, $\le 35\% VO_2^{max}$ for 8-hr shifts.
- **Strength vs. Endurance**: Strength is the **maximum one-time force**; endurance is the **ability to sustain**; **Rohmert's curve** governs endurance vs. force: $t = k \cdot (MVC/F)^p$.
- **MMH** includes lift, lower, push, pull, carry, hold — the dominant cause of occupational back injury.
- **NIOSH Lifting Equation**: $RWL = 23 \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$, all six multipliers always required.
- **Multipliers**:
  - $HM = 25/H$ ($H$ in cm)
  - $VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$ ($V$ in cm)
  - $DM = 0.82 + 4.5/D$ ($D$ in cm)
  - $AM = 1 - 0.0032A$ ($A$ in degrees)
  - $FM$ from NIOSH frequency table (lifts/min × duration)
  - $CM \in \{1.0, 0.95, 0.90\}$
- **Lifting Index**: $LI = L / RWL$; thresholds $\le 1.0$, $(1, 3]$, $> 3.0$ for low/moderate/high risk.
- **Reducing MMH overexertion risk** — Hierarchy: **Eliminate $\rightarrow$ Engineering $\rightarrow$ Administrative $\rightarrow$ PPE/Training**.
- **Biomechanical design principles** — minimize moment arms, use large muscle groups, neutral joints, avoid static load, allow recovery, match demand to capability.
- **Anthropometry** — design for **5th to 95th percentile** to accommodate 90% of the user population; separate **static (structural)** vs. **dynamic (functional)** dimensions.
- **HR max** estimate: $220 - \text{age}$; **VO₂ max** is the gold standard for aerobic fitness; **5.0 kcal/L O₂** is the standard energy conversion.
- **RPE (Borg Scale)** is a subjective but validated strain indicator; useful for field screening.
- **LC = 23 kg** is the reference constant; do not confuse with a "safe load for all conditions" — real-world RWL is always less.
- **Always check units**: cm for $H, V, D$; degrees for $A$; kg for $L$ and RWL; L/min for VO₂; kcal/min or W for energy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT832 - HUMAN FACTORS IN ENGINEERING AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Anthropometric Design Principles; Static and Dynamic Anthropometry; Case Study of Anthropometric Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — ANTHROPOMETRIC DESIGN PRINCIPLES, STATIC & DYNAMIC ANTHROPOMETRY

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Anthropometry

**Anthropometry** is the scientific study of the measurement and proportions of the human body. In the context of Human Factors Engineering (HFE), it deals with the systematic collection of body-size data — including linear dimensions, depths, breadths, circumferences, weights, and ranges of motion — and the application of this data to the design of equipment, products, and workplaces.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology, anthropometry is treated as the *quantitative bridge* between the biological variability of the human user population and the geometric specifications of engineered systems. It is a **sub-discipline of ergonomics** that converts biological variability into engineering tolerances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anthropometry is NOT just "body measurement"** — it is the *engineering translation* of those measurements into design specifications (clearances, reaches, seat heights, door widths, etc.). The 2024 Scheme explicitly emphasizes the *application* aspect for board-level answers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Master Tailor**
> Imagine you are a master tailor in 1900, asked to make a single military uniform for an entire regiment of 1,000 soldiers — men of different heights, shoulder widths, and arm lengths. What do you do? You measure a *sample*, find the *most common sizes*, and either (a) make one size that "fits most" or (b) introduce **adjustable straps and belts** to absorb the variability.
>
> That is **exactly** what an ergonomist does with anthropometry. The "uniform" is the workstation, vehicle, phone, or chair; the "regiment" is the user population; the "straps" are **adjustability, telescopic ranges, and modular components**. The "measurements" are static and dynamic dimensions.

### 1.3 Sub-Classification of Anthropometry

Anthropometric data is divided into two major categories that the KTU board regularly tests:

| Category | Alternate Name | What is Measured | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static Anthropometry** | Structural / Static Dimensions | Body in *fixed, standardized* postures | Door heights, ceiling clearances, seat lengths |
| **Dynamic Anthropometry** | Functional / Dynamic Dimensions | Body *in motion* (reaches, clearances during activity) | Pedal placement, control reach envelopes, walking paths |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Types?** A door does not care whether you are walking or running — it needs to clear your *static* head height. But the steering wheel of a car must clear your shoulders when you *turn* to reverse — that is a *dynamic* (functional) clearance. The same human body yields very different design numbers depending on the activity.

### 1.4 Key Physical Standards & Constants (Bolded)

* **Percentile range commonly used in design:** **5th percentile (P5) to 95th percentile (P95)** — covers **90%** of the target population.
* **Standard reference posture:** **Erect standing posture** for static measurements (ANSI/HFES 100-2007).
* **Standard deviation multiplier for normal distribution:** **Z = 1.645** for the 90% accommodation band (P5 and P95).
* **Acceleration of gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** — used in dynamic body-segment inertial calculations.
* **Body-segment mass fractions (Dempster's coefficients):** Head **8.1%**, Trunk **49.7%**, Upper Arm **2.8%**, Forearm **1.6%**, Hand **0.6%**, Thigh **9.9%**, Calf **4.6%**, Foot **1.4%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Anthropometric Design (The 6 Pillars)

The KTU syllabus demands a clear articulation of the **design principles** that govern how anthropometric data is applied. There are six universally accepted principles:

### Principle 1 — *Design for the Range, Not the Average*
The human body is *highly variable* across populations. Designing for the **mean** will exclude roughly **50%** of users (those on either tail). The KTU board wants students to quote: *"Design for the 5th to 95th percentile population."*

### Principle 2 — *Identify the Critical Dimension First*
Not all dimensions are equally important. A designer must identify the **critical (limiting) dimension** — the dimension that, if mis-sized, will cause the design to fail functionally. Example: In a car seat, **buttock-to-knee length** is critical for leg clearance.

### Principle 3 — *Distinguish Clearance vs. Reach Dimensions*
* **Clearance dimensions** (e.g., overhead clearance, legroom) — design for the **largest** user (P95 or higher).
* **Reach dimensions** (e.g., control reach, brake pedal distance) — design for the **smallest** user (P5 or lower).
> This is a *favourite* KTU Part A question.

### Principle 4 — *Provide Adjustability Where Critical Range Exceeds 5th–95th*
If the 5th–95th range is so large that a single fixed dimension would be unsafe or unusable, the design must incorporate **adjustability** (e.g., telescoping steering column, sliding seat).

### Principle 5 — *Account for Clothing, PPE, and Posture Distortion*
* Add **~10 mm** to clearance dimensions for normal clothing.
* Add **~25–40 mm** for heavy work clothing or PPE (helmets, boots, gloves).
* A seated person has **+20 to +30 mm** in vertical reach compared to a standing person.

### Principle 6 — *Use Population-Specific Data — Never Mix Populations*
Male data cannot be applied to female populations, and adult data cannot be used for children. KTU explicitly tests this in case studies (e.g., cockpit design must use *aircrew-specific* data, not general population data).

## 2.2 Static Anthropometry — Detailed Technical Description

### 2.2.1 Definition (Exam-Ready)
**Static anthropometry** is the measurement of the human body in *immobile, standardized, anatomically defined postures* (e.g., standing erect with heels together, sitting upright with thighs horizontal).

### 2.2.2 Standardized Reference Postures

| Posture | Code | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Erect standing | **P1** | Maximum vertical dimensions, doorway heights |
| Sitting (knees 90°) | **P2** | Workstation heights, seat depths |
| Sitting (knees 110°–115°) | **P3** | Automotive, aircraft (relaxed posture) |
| Crouched | **P4** | Tank crew, mine workers |
| Supine | **P5** | Medical (MRI, stretcher) design |

### 2.2.3 Common Static Dimensions (With Notation)

* $H_{st}$ — Stature (standing height)
* $H_{si}$ — Sitting height (vertex of head to seat surface)
* $L_{bk}$ — Buttock-to-knee length
* $L_{kp}$ — Knee-to-popliteal length
* $W_{sh}$ — Shoulder (biacromial) breadth
* $W_{hi}$ — Hip (bi-iliac) breadth
* $D_{ch}$ — Chest depth
* $L_{fa}$ — Forearm-to-fingertip length
* $H_{kp}$ — Knee height (sitting)
* $D_{th}$ — Thigh clearance (above seat)

> [!NOTE]
> **Static measurements are repeatable, reliable, and statistically easy to analyse.** That is why they were the *first* anthropometric data collected historically (e.g., the 1950 US Air Force survey of 4,000 personnel).

## 2.3 Dynamic (Functional) Anthropometry — Detailed Technical Description

### 2.3.1 Definition
**Dynamic anthropometry** measures the body (or body segments) **in motion** or during functional reach. It captures *envelopes of movement* rather than fixed lengths.

### 2.3.2 Common Dynamic Dimensions

* $R_{fwd}$ — Forward functional reach (seated, fingertip to backrest, horizontal)
* $R_{up}$ — Upward reach (vertical, fingertip to floor, standing)
* $R_{ovh}$ — Overhead reach (vertical with shoulder flexion)
* $C_{leg}$ — Legroom clearance (forward-backward during pedal operation)
* $C_{head}$ — Head clearance (during head turning and helmet rotation)
* $B_{seat}$ — Seat slide range (forwards to fully reclined)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic measurements require motion-tracking equipment** — goniometers, optical/infrared motion capture (Vicon, OptiTrack), or even simple potentiometer-based linkage devices. The *output* is a **clearance envelope** — a 3-D boundary that the body sweeps during activity.

### 2.3.3 Why Static Data Fails for Dynamic Tasks

* A person's **forward reach while seated** is **>200 mm longer** than their forward reach while standing (due to spine flexion).
* **Overhead reach** is often **>300 mm greater** than the static fingertip height.
* **Eye rotation** (without head movement) is limited to ~±15° horizontal and ~±10° vertical — this drives the design of *display panels* (e.g., cockpit HUDs).
* A 5th-percentile woman has a *seated functional reach* of only ~**700 mm**, while a 95th-percentile man reaches ~**900 mm** — a **200 mm spread** that must be absorbed by seat adjustment.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU ESE.** Boards have a 75–80% probability of testing percentile/clearance calculations.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Percentile (ungrouped, ranked) | $P = \dfrac{N - 0.5}{M} \times 100$ | Raw ranked data |
| 2 | Percentile (grouped data) | $P = L + \dfrac{\dfrac{NP}{100} - F}{f} \cdot h$ | Frequency distribution |
| 3 | Z-score (normal dist.) | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Convert X to percentile |
| 4 | Design value at P% | $X_{P} = \mu \pm Z_{P} \cdot \sigma$ | Design reach/clearance |
| 5 | 5th–95th range | $R_{5-95} = 2 \times 1.645 \times \sigma = 3.29\sigma$ | Accommodation band width |
| 6 | Mean (grouped) | $\mu = \dfrac{\sum f_i \cdot x_i}{\sum f_i}$ | Central tendency |
| 7 | Standard deviation (grouped) | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum f_i (x_i - \mu)^2}{\sum f_i}}$ | Variability |
| 8 | Body segment length (ratio) | $L_{seg} = r \cdot H_{st}$ | Proportion-based estimates |
| 9 | Body segment mass (Dempster) | $m_{seg} = c_{seg} \cdot M_{body}$ | Dynamic force analysis |
| 10 | Functional reach (seated, fwd) | $R_{fwd} \approx 1.20 \cdot L_{fa} + H_{si}$ | Workstation layout |
| 11 | Clearance add-on (PPE) | $C_{design} = X_{95} + 2 \cdot PPE$ | Safety margins |
| 12 | Population range index | $PRI = \dfrac{P_{95} - P_{5}}{\mu} \times 100\%$ | Comparative variability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Table Syntax:** In the table above, vertical bar delimiters are *intentionally absent* (the table uses cell boundaries only). For inline math, use $\vert X - \mu \vert$ instead of $\vert X - \mu \vert$ in raw text.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Automotive interior design** uses *combined* static + dynamic anthropometry: seat height (static), pedal reach envelopes (dynamic), head clearance with helmet (dynamic), steering column range (adjustable design).
* **Aerospace cockpit design** uses aircrew-specific data (5th–95th percentile USAF aircrew, not general population) — failure to do so caused the early F-16 ejection seat fatalities where the 5th-percentile female load exceeded spinal tolerance.
* **Consumer electronics (smartphone)** uses hand breadth and thumb-reach data — this is why a "phablet" with 6.7-inch screen is *unusable single-handedly* for users with 5th-percentile hand breadth.
* **Disability-inclusive design** extends P5 even further down to P1 (wheelchair footrest reach) and the *Anthropometry of Disability* handbook (US Access Board).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Percentile from Grouped Frequency Data

The grouped-data percentile formula is the most-tested in KTU ESE. We derive it carefully.

### Starting Point — Cumulative Frequency

Define for a continuous frequency distribution with class width $h$:

* $L$ = lower class boundary of the percentile class
* $F$ = cumulative frequency *before* the percentile class
* $f$ = frequency *within* the percentile class
* $N$ = total population
* $P$ = desired percentile

We seek the value $X$ such that exactly $NP/100$ observations fall at or below $X$.

### Linear Interpolation Inside the Class

Assume observations are **uniformly distributed** within the percentile class. Then the *fraction* of the class needed is:

$$
\text{fraction} = \dfrac{\dfrac{NP}{100} - F}{f}
$$

Multiplying this fraction by the class width $h$ gives the distance *into* the class from its lower boundary:

$$
\Delta X = h \cdot \dfrac{\dfrac{NP}{100} - F}{f}
$$

Adding this to the lower boundary $L$ yields the percentile value:

$$
\boxed{P = L + \dfrac{\dfrac{NP}{100} - F}{f} \cdot h}
$$

> **Why linear interpolation?** Within a narrow class of, say, 10 mm, the human body distribution is *approximately* linear. For higher accuracy (KTU honours level), one would fit a Gaussian or use kernel density estimation, but the linear model is the KTU board standard.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Design Value at a Given Percentile (Normal Distribution)

For ergonomic design, body dimensions are usually approximated as **normally distributed** with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. The Z-score tells us how many standard deviations a value lies from the mean.

### Step 1 — Define the Z-score
$$
Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for X
Multiply both sides by $\sigma$:
$$
Z \cdot \sigma = X - \mu
$$

### Step 3 — Isolate X (the design value)
$$
X = \mu + Z \cdot \sigma
$$

### Step 4 — Sign Convention
* For **upper** percentiles (P50 → P95, P99): use **positive** $Z$.
* For **lower** percentiles (P5, P1): use **negative** $Z$.

$$
\boxed{X_{P} = \mu \pm Z_{P} \cdot \sigma}
$$

### Common Z-values (KTU must-know)

| Percentile | $Z_{P}$ |
|---|---|
| P1   | $-2.326$ |
| P5   | $-1.645$ |
| P10  | $-1.282$ |
| P25  | $-0.674$ |
| P50  | $0.000$ |
| P75  | $+0.674$ |
| P90  | $+1.282$ |
| P95  | $+1.645$ |
| P99  | $+2.326$ |

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The 5th-to-95th Accommodation Range

The **design accommodation range** $R_{5-95}$ is the spread between the 5th and 95th percentiles — the designer must accommodate this whole range.

### Step 1 — Write both percentile values
$$
X_{95} = \mu + 1.645 \sigma
$$
$$
X_{5} = \mu - 1.645 \sigma
$$

### Step 2 — Subtract
$$
R_{5-95} = X_{95} - X_{5} = (\mu + 1.645 \sigma) - (\mu - 1.645 \sigma)
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify
The $\mu$ terms cancel:
$$
R_{5-95} = 1.645 \sigma + 1.645 \sigma = 2 \times 1.645 \sigma
$$

### Step 4 — Final numerical form
$$
\boxed{R_{5-95} = 3.29 \cdot \sigma}
$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** *The 5th–95th range is always exactly 3.29 standard deviations wide.* This is independent of the dimension measured — a beautiful property of the normal distribution that designers exploit to set **adjustment ranges** for any control.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (Board-Standard Format)

**Problem:** The sitting knee height of 1,000 male factory workers is normally distributed with mean $\mu = 540$ mm and standard deviation $\sigma = 28$ mm. An ergonomist is designing a workstation where the seat must accommodate the 5th–95th percentile population.

**Find:** (i) The 5th and 95th percentile knee heights.
(ii) The required seat-height adjustment range.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Identify percentile Z-values**
From the table: $Z_{5} = -1.645$ and $Z_{95} = +1.645$.

**Step 2 — Calculate the 5th percentile**
$$
X_{5} = \mu + Z_{5} \cdot \sigma = 540 + (-1.645)(28)
$$
$$
X_{5} = 540 - 46.06 = 493.94 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the 95th percentile**
$$
X_{95} = \mu + Z_{95} \cdot \sigma = 540 + (1.645)(28)
$$
$$
X_{95} = 540 + 46.06 = 586.06 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Adjustment range**
$$
R_{5-95} = X_{95} - X_{5} = 586.06 - 493.94 = 92.12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Verification using the closed form:**
$$
R_{5-95} = 3.29 \cdot \sigma = 3.29 \times 28 = 92.12 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

> **Conclusion:** The seat must be adjustable from **494 mm to 586 mm** — a range of **~92 mm** — to accommodate 90% of the worker population.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Static vs. Dynamic

| Design Decision Context | Use Static Data? | Use Dynamic Data? | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ceiling height in a room | **Yes** | No | Activity is upright standing; no motion envelope needed |
| Cockpit ejection seat | Yes (body dims) | **Yes** (ejection envelope) | Body must clear the canopy during ejection |
| Doorway width | **Yes** | No (mostly) | Static shoulder breadth suffices |
| Car dashboard reach | No | **Yes** | Driver's reach envelope matters |
| Office chair backrest | Yes (lumbar) | **Yes** (reclining motion) | Reclining changes lumbar contact |
| Aircraft ejection trajectory | No | **Yes** (4-D) | Pure motion analysis (sled track) |
| Mobile phone width | **Yes** | Yes (thumb reach) | Hand grip + thumb envelope |
| MRI bore diameter | **Yes** | No | Patient lies static |

## 3.6 Body-Segment Inertial Dynamics (Brief)

For dynamic design (e.g., crash safety, exoskeletons), designers use **Dempster's body-segment coefficients** $c_i$. The mass of the i-th segment is:

$$
m_i = c_i \cdot M_{body}
$$

The centre-of-mass location of segment $i$ (from proximal joint) is at fraction $p_i$ of segment length. The moment of inertia about that joint is:

$$
I_i = c_i \cdot M_{body} \cdot (p_i \cdot L_i)^2
$$

These enter equations such as the **angular momentum** of a body segment during a movement:

$$
H_i = I_i \cdot \omega_i
$$

where $\omega_i$ is the angular velocity. KTU honours questions may quote this.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Anthropometric Design Process

This flowchart shows the **complete process** an ergonomist follows from data collection to validated design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Identify User Population] --> A2[Step 2: Collect Anthropometric Data Static AND Dynamic]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Compute mu and sigma for Critical Dimensions]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Decide Accommodation Goal e.g. P5 to P95]
    A4 --> A5{Step 5: Is Single Fixed Size Feasible?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Step 6A: Design at One Percentile Reach at P5 Clearance at P95]
    A5 -- No --> A7[Step 6B: Provide Adjustability Across 3.29 sigma Range]
    A6 --> A8[Step 7: Add PPE and Clothing Margin 10 to 40 mm]
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9[Step 8: Build Prototype and Test]
    A9 --> A10[Step 9: Validate via Usability Trials with 5th and 95th Percentile Users]
    A10 --> A11[Step 10: Iterate and Finalize Design]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Static vs Dynamic Anthropometry Architecture

This shows the *functional architecture* of how static and dynamic measurements feed into different design subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Source: Human Body]
        H1[Static Postures: Standing, Sitting, Crouching]
        H2[Dynamic Actions: Reach, Lift, Twist, Walk]
    end

    subgraph MEAS[Measurement Phase]
        M1[Stadiometer, Anthropometer, Calipers]
        M2[Motion Capture, Goniometer, 3D Scanner]
    end

    subgraph DATA[Data Phase]
        D1[Static Dimensional Database e.g. Stature, Sitting Height]
        D2[Dynamic Envelope Database e.g. Reach, Clearance, Swept Volume]
    end

    subgraph APPL[Application Phase]
        APP1[Fixed Design: Doors, Ceilings, Bed Lengths]
        APP2[Workspace Layout: Cockpits, Control Rooms, Workstations]
        APP3[Safety Margins: Ejection, Crash, PPE Clearance]
        APP4[Adjustable Design: Seats, Steering, Pedals, Tools]
    end

    H1 --> M1 --> D1
    H2 --> M2 --> D2
    D1 --> APP1
    D1 --> APP4
    D2 --> APP2
    D2 --> APP3
    D1 --> APP2
    D2 --> APP4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anthropometric Design Decision Tree

This block diagram maps the **decision logic** an ergonomist uses for each dimension.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Design Dimension Specified] --> Q1{Is it a Clearance Dimension?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q1A[Design at P95 to P99 of Population]
    Q1A --> Q1B[Add PPE Margin 25 to 40 mm]
    Q1B --> Q1C[Final Clearance Value]

    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is it a Reach Dimension?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q2A[Design at P5 of Population]
    Q2A --> Q2B[Add Reach Efficiency Factor 0.85]
    Q2B --> Q2C[Final Reach Value]

    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is it a Strength or Force Dimension?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q3A[Design for P5 of Strength P95 of Body Size]
    Q3A --> Q3C[Final Strength Value]

    Q3 -- No --> Q4[Use Population Mean mu with 1 sigma Tolerance]
    Q4 --> Q4C[Final Average Value]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (A1, M2, Q1B, etc.). No reserved keywords (end, subgraph, graph, style) are used as node names. All labels are uppercase alphanumeric text only — no bold, italics, or HTML inside node labels.
